atlantic-france.metaDesc: Charter routes through South Brittany: Belle-Île, Houat, Hoëdic and the Gulf of Morbihan. Wind, tides and island anchorages, day by day.
atlantic-france.title: Sail South Brittany's Islands and Tides
atlantic-france.description: This corner of the Atlantic rewards sailors who read the water. Summer winds usually settle into a moderate southwesterly, Force 3 or 4 most afternoons, which makes for easy reaches between islands that rarely sit more than a couple of hours apart. Quiberon Bay stays sheltered enough for a relaxed first day, then the coast opens up. Belle-Île is the big draw, its cliffs and the Grotte de l'Apothicairerie on the wild side, Le Palais and its Vauban citadel on the calmer one. Smaller Houat and Hoëdic offer white-sand anchorages and almost no cars. The Gulf of Morbihan is the puzzle everyone remembers, an inland sea threaded with islets like Île aux Moines and Île d'Arz. One rule matters here: time your run through the Gulf mouth with the flood or slack, since the Jument current can top nine knots at springs and will stop a cruising yacht dead. July and August fill Le Palais by late afternoon, so arrive before three or aim for June and September.

corsica.metaDesc: Day-by-day Corsica sailing routes from Ajaccio to Bonifacio and the Lavezzi reserve, with wind, distances and the best months to dodge the crowds.
corsica.title: Sail Corsica's Granite Coast and Reserve Bays
corsica.description: Most crews base out of Ajaccio and work south toward Bonifacio, a run that rewards you with steady thermal winds of 10 to 20 knots through summer afternoons. The sailing here is honest work: the west coast throws up force 3 to 6 westerlies, and the Strait of Bonifacio funnels the Libeccio into short, sharp gusts, so carry a plan B for the crossing out to the Îles Lavezzi. That reserve of granite islets and shallow turquoise bays is the payoff. North of Ajaccio, the red porphyry cliffs of Scandola and the boat-only hamlet of Girolata make a quieter alternative to the southern circuit. Legs mostly run 15 to 30 nautical miles, easy day-hops. One practical note: Bonifacio's fjord harbour packs out from mid-July through August, so radio ahead for a berth or anchor off and tender in. Come in June or September and the same anchorages feel half-empty.

cote-azur.metaDesc: Day-by-day charter routes on the Côte d'Azur: light winds, short hops from Cannes to Saint-Tropez, Port-Cros and Porquerolles' island anchorages.
cote-azur.title: From Cannes Glamour to Wild Island Anchorages
cote-azur.description: The Côte d'Azur packs short, forgiving hops into a coastline that swings from film-festival marinas to pine-covered islands in a single afternoon. Winds are usually light, a westerly of 10 to 25 knots through the day that eases after dark, with almost no tide to plan around. Saint-Tropez sits about 26 nautical miles from Cannes, so you rarely log long passages. Point the bow at the Îles de Lérins for a free anchorage off Saint-Honorat's monastery, then push west to Port-Cros, the first national park in Europe to protect land and seabed together. Porquerolles rewards the detour with white-sand bays like Plage Notre-Dame. If you want the glamour without the gridlock, sail here in late May, June, or September; July and August turn Saint-Tropez into a superyacht car park. One local habit worth copying: check the holding before you drop, since anchoring over the protected Posidonia seagrass is both illegal and poor ground.

french-canals.metaDesc: Licence-free canal cruising across France: the Canal du Midi, Alsace's Arzviller boat lift and Burgundy's Saône. 7- and 14-day routes.
french-canals.title: Cruise France's Canals at Walking Pace
french-canals.description: Forget sails and tide tables. On France's inland waterways you travel at walking pace behind an engine, measuring each day in cruising hours and lock counts rather than nautical miles. No licence is needed, and an hour of dockside training covers the basics. Three very different stretches sit under this banner. The Canal du Midi runs plane-tree tunnels past medieval Carcassonne and the Minervois vineyards around Le Somail and Homps. In Alsace, the Canal de la Marne au Rhin climbs the Saint-Louis-Arzviller boat lift, which raises your hull 45 metres in a single move, then threads the castle-studded Zorn valley to Saverne. Burgundy trades narrow locks for the broad, open Saône, with Saint-Jean-de-Losne as its long-standing barging hub. Boats run roughly April to October; book the quieter shoulder weeks if July crowds at popular moorings put you off. Bring a bike, because the towpaths make for easy shore rides between stops.

ajaccio-south-corsica.metaDesc: Sail south from Ajaccio to Bonifacio and the Lavezzi islands: seven days through the Gulf of Valinco, granite towers and cliff-top harbours.

1.shortDescription: Slip out of Napoleon's home port, round Capo di Muro, and drop the hook in the sandy coves of Porto Pollo.
1.description: Provisioning in Ajaccio is easy, so most crews load up at the covered market before casting off. Once clear of the gulf the coast turns wilder as you work south past Capo di Muro, roughly twenty miles of open water with the afternoon thermal usually filling in behind you. Porto Pollo sits at the mouth of the Gulf of Valinco, a low-key spot with a sandy bottom that holds an anchor well. The harbour office is genuinely helpful and answers the radio, which counts for a lot when you arrive late. Swim off the back before dinner; the water here is calm and shallow enough for a first night. We usually leave Ajaccio by mid-morning to reach the anchorage with light to spare and time for a proper shakedown of the boat.
thingsToDo: Shop Ajaccio's covered market before casting off
Snorkel the shallows off Porto Pollo
Rent a windsurfer in Porto Pollo bay
Sundowner at a Porto Pollo beach bar
1.mooringTip: Porto Pollo has good holding on sand in 3-5 metres; anchor off the beach rather than crowding the small jetty, and hail the harbour office on VHF if you want a lazy line or a berth in July.

2.shortDescription: A short two-hour hop across the Valinco to Campomoro, where the largest Genoese tower in Corsica guards a quiet sandy bay.
2.description: This is one of the shorter legs of the week, barely two hours if the breeze cooperates, so there's no rush to leave. Campomoro closes off the southern side of the Gulf of Valinco, a village of granite houses that has kept its fishing-hamlet feel. The star-shaped tower on the headland is the biggest in Corsica and worth the twenty-minute walk up for the view back over the gulf. Anchoring is straightforward in settled weather, with good sand off the beach. Keep an eye on the forecast, though: the bay opens to the southwest, and a libeccio will send swell straight in. Snorkelling along the rocks at the northern end turns up plenty of fish. Ashore, a handful of small restaurants serve the day's catch a few steps from the water.
thingsToDo: Climb to the star-shaped Campomoro tower
Snorkel the rocks at the bay's north end
Follow the coastal path past the tower
Fresh catch at a beachfront restaurant
2.mooringTip: Anchor on sand off the beach in 4-6 metres, but treat Campomoro as fair-weather only. If a southwesterly libeccio is forecast overnight the bay gets rolly, and Porto Pollo or Propriano make safer boltholes.

3.shortDescription: Coast south to Tizzano, a pocket-sized fishing port on a clear-water cove, roughly halfway to the Bonifacio strait.
3.description: South of Campomoro the coast grows remote, backed by the maquis of the Senetosa reserve with its twin lighthouses. Tizzano appears as a narrow inlet, easy to miss until you're almost on it. The port itself takes boats under ten metres; anything larger anchors off the pier in the cove, where the water runs clear over sand and weed. It's a small place, a scatter of houses, a grocery for topping up, and a couple of restaurants that lean hard on the local catch and Sartene wine. Not much happens here, which is rather the point after a couple of busy harbours. We often time this leg for late morning so the afternoon sun is on the anchorage. Take the dinghy ashore for an early dinner and watch the fishing boats come in.
thingsToDo: Anchor and swim in Tizzano's clear cove
Fresh fish dinner at the port
Pair supper with a Sartene red
Top up at the Tizzano grocery
3.mooringTip: The tiny port only suits boats under about 10 m; larger yachts anchor off the jetty in 3-5 m on sand. Set the hook well and check your swing, as the cove is snug and fills with local moorings in season.

4.shortDescription: Round Corsica's southwest tip and thread the cliff-slot entrance into Bonifacio, one of the Mediterranean's great natural harbours.
4.description: Today's leg carries the most exposure of the trip, rounding the southwest corner where the Strait of Bonifacio funnels wind between Corsica and Sardinia. A libeccio or mistral can pipe up past thirty knots, so we always check the forecast the night before and leave early to beat the afternoon build. The reward is the approach. From seaward the entrance is a plain slit in white limestone cliffs, invisible until you're close, then the calanque doglegs right and the harbour opens beneath the old town. Bonifacio clings to the clifftop above, all tall houses and Genoese ramparts. The marina fills fast in July and August, so book ahead. Once tied up, climb the stairs into the haute ville for a drink on the ramparts and the walk out to the Bosco cemetery at sunset.
thingsToDo: Walk the ramparts of the haute ville
Descend the King of Aragon staircase
Sunset at the Bosco clifftop cemetery
Aperitif on a Bonifacio marina terrace
4.mooringTip: Book the Bonifacio marina ahead for July and August; it's a stern-to berth on the quay with lazy lines and a harbour team on the pontoons. If it's full, the Calanque de la Catena buoys just outside cost around 25 euros for a monohull.

5.shortDescription: A six-mile skip across the strait to the Lavezzi islands, a granite nature reserve ringed with pale sand and snorkelling coves.
5.description: The Lavezzi archipelago lies just a few miles east of Bonifacio, close enough that timing is all about wind rather than distance. These are low granite islands, weathered into rounded boulders, and the whole group sits inside the Bocche di Bonifacio nature reserve. Cala di Greco on the north side is the usual anchorage, with a sandy patch to drop into and buoys nearby that are reserved for the tripper boats running out from Bonifacio, so leave those clear. The snorkelling around Cala Lazarina and Cala di u Grecu is the best of the week, granite gullies alive with fish in gin-clear shallows. Watch the marked no-anchor zones, fenced off with yellow buoys to protect the seagrass. This is a daytime stop; the anchorage is open, so most crews move back to shelter before nightfall.
thingsToDo: Snorkel the granite gullies at Cala Lazarina
Anchor and swim off Cala di Greco
Walk the trail to the Lavezzi memorials
Watch for fish in Cala di u Grecu
5.mooringTip: Anchor on the sand patches at Cala di Greco in 3-6 m and stay well off the yellow-buoyed protection zones. It's a fair-weather day anchorage only, exposed to swell, so plan to be back in Bonifacio or heading north before evening.

6.shortDescription: The long haul north back up the west coast to Propriano, a proper resort marina at the head of the Gulf of Valinco.
6.description: Here's the big mile-eater of the week, a long beat or reach north from the strait back into the Gulf of Valinco. Get away early. With the prevailing southwesterly you may carry a decent sailing angle much of the way, but the leg is exposed and best done in the morning's lighter air rather than the afternoon thermal at full strength. Propriano waits at the top of the gulf, a proper resort town with a big marina, cafes and restaurants lining the quay, and a harbour master who takes you onto the pontoon. After the wild anchorages of the south it feels almost busy. Reprovision here, do the laundry, and eat ashore; the seafront restaurants do good grilled fish and there's real choice. It's a comfortable, sheltered berth to regroup before the final run home.
thingsToDo: Reprovision along the Propriano quay
Grilled fish at a seafront restaurant
Evening stroll the marina promenade
Swim at Propriano's Plage du Lido
6.mooringTip: Call Propriano's capitainerie on approach for a berth; the harbour team meets you on the pontoon, with water and power laid on. It's a well-sheltered marina, a good place to sit out any libeccio brewing offshore.

7.shortDescription: The final leg back to base, tracing the Valinco coast and rounding Capo di Muro to close the loop at Ajaccio.
7.description: The run home retraces the exposed headland at Capo di Muro before the coast eases into the Gulf of Ajaccio. It's around thirty miles, so leave in good time, especially if you have a fixed hand-back slot; charter bases usually want the boat back by evening or the following morning. If the timing is kind there's a case for one last swim stop in a cove short of the city, Portigliolo or the beaches below Coti-Chiavari, before the marina bustle. Ajaccio itself deserves an afternoon if you have it. The old town and Napoleon's birthplace, the citadel, and a last dinner ashore round the week off well. We tend to fuel up and settle the boat the evening before hand-back so the final morning is unhurried and nobody's chasing the clock.
thingsToDo: Last swim at Portigliolo beach
Tour Napoleon's birthplace, Maison Bonaparte
Wander Ajaccio's old town and citadel
Farewell dinner on the Ajaccio quay
7.mooringTip: Refuel before returning to the Ajaccio charter berth, and confirm your hand-back time and pontoon with the base. Aim to be alongside with daylight to spare; the marina gets busy at turnaround and manoeuvring room is tight.

alsace-canal-week.metaDesc: A relaxed 7-day canal loop from Hesse through the Arzviller boat lift, Lutzelbourg and Saverne, then west past the Gondrexange lakes to Lagarde.

1.shortDescription: A short shakedown hop east from the Hesse base to Niderviller, home to a faience works that has fired porcelain for centuries.
1.description: The first day is deliberately gentle. From the port at Hesse you drop the lines mid-morning, ease past the old Benedictine abbey that now serves as the village church, and settle into canal pace, which is walking pace with a coffee in hand. Two or three locks separate you from Niderviller, enough to get the crew working the ropes without any pressure. Niderviller earns its stop. The faience and porcelain manufactory here has been turning out tableware since the eighteenth century, and its shop is a short walk from the quay. The village keeps its window boxes full through summer. Tie up early, stretch your legs, and let everyone learn where the fenders live before tomorrow's big day at the Arzviller lift.
thingsToDo: Tour the Niderviller faience and porcelain works
Buy hand-painted tableware at the manufactory shop
Walk to the Benedictine abbey church in Hesse
Practice lock handling on the quiet first locks
1.mooringTip: Niderviller's port de plaisance has a sheet-piling quay on the south side with water and power; the Neubruch quay just downstream takes about fifteen boats if the main basin fills up. Arrive by mid-afternoon in July.

2.shortDescription: The headline day: two canal tunnels and the Arzviller boat lift drop you 45 metres down to Lutzelbourg, huddled under its ruined castle.
2.description: Leave early. The Arzviller lift runs on a timetable and boats gather to descend in batches, so an unhurried breakfast beats a long wait at the top. Before you reach it, two tunnels test the nerves: the short Niderviller bore at 475 metres, then the long Arzviller tunnel at over 2,300 metres, lit but narrow, where you creep through in single file. Then comes the reason people book this canal. The Saint-Louis-Arzviller inclined plane carries your boat sideways down a 45-metre slope in a water-filled caisson, doing in minutes what seventeen old locks once took a full day to manage. Lutzelbourg waits below, wooden houses stacked under a broken castle keep. The Fournil des Écluisiers bakery does the morning croissants.
thingsToDo: Ride the Saint-Louis-Arzviller inclined plane caisson
Motor through the 2,300-metre Arzviller tunnel
Climb to Lutzelbourg's ruined castle keep
Grab croissants at Fournil des Écluisiers
2.mooringTip: Lutzelbourg has a popular quay right in the village; in peak weeks it fills by early afternoon, so slot in before the lift's later boats arrive. Bollards line the bank for an easy step-off, and there is no need to reserve.

3.shortDescription: Three easy hours down the wooded Zorn valley land you at Saverne, moored in the basin below the pink sandstone Château des Rohan.
3.description: Short and scenic. The run down to Saverne takes about three hours through the wooded Zorn valley, a handful of locks breaking up the green. We usually leave by nine to have the afternoon free in town. Saverne rewards the walk. Moor in the wide basin and the Château des Rohan sits right across the water, a 140-metre sweep of pink sandstone they call the little Versailles of Alsace. The old town runs to half-timbered fronts and geranium boxes. If your legs are willing, the ruins of Haut-Barr sit on a ridge above town, the so-called Eye of Alsace, with views reaching toward Strasbourg and the Rhine on a clear day. For dinner, the Taverne Katz occupies a carved sixteenth-century house near the church.
thingsToDo: Tour the pink sandstone Château des Rohan
Climb to the Haut-Barr ruins for the view
Wander Saverne's rose garden in bloom
Dine at the sixteenth-century Taverne Katz
3.mooringTip: The basin opposite the château is the prize berth and fills fast on summer afternoons; there is water and electricity, with a capitainerie on the quay. Aim to be tied up by two if you want a château view from the cockpit.

4.shortDescription: Retrace the Zorn valley back to Lutzelbourg, a slow half-day that leaves the afternoon for the castle path and the crystal workshops.
4.description: The same valley, read backwards, looks different. You climb rather than descend the locks today, and the light falls the other way across the wooded slopes. It is a genuine half-day, so there is no reason to rush the ropes. Plenty of crews break the run and tie up on a quiet stretch of bank for lunch in open country, which is one of the real pleasures of this canal. Back at Lutzelbourg, the footpath up to the castle ruin takes about twenty minutes and pays off with a long view down the valley you have just cruised. The village keeps a couple of small workshops trading on the area's glass and crystal heritage. Evening is for the terrace, a local Riesling, and an early night before the lift again.
thingsToDo: Moor mid-canal for a countryside lunch
Hike the footpath to Lutzelbourg castle ruin
Browse the village crystal and glass workshops
Sip Alsace Riesling on the quay terrace
4.mooringTip: Anywhere the bank is piled or firm you can moor with stakes and a mallet in open country, so carry a couple of spare mooring pins. Back in Lutzelbourg the village quay is first-come, and an earlier arrival secures the good spots.

5.shortDescription: Back up the boat lift and west past your home base to Xouaxange, where the canal threads between medieval monks' fishponds and the Gondrexange lake.
5.description: Today you turn west and change scenery for good. The inclined plane lifts you back to the summit, the two tunnels pass in reverse, and beyond Niderviller the canal leaves the wooded gorge for open Lorraine. You will slide straight past Hesse without stopping, which can feel slightly odd on a round trip. The character softens out here. Water spreads into big shallow lakes, the étangs that Benedictine monks dug in the Middle Ages to farm carp, and reed beds replace the tree walls of Alsace. Xouaxange announces itself with a low stone bridge over the cut. Gondrexange, the largest of the lakes, sits just beyond, good for a swim off the boat or an hour with a rod. It is a quiet, birdy stretch, best taken slowly.
thingsToDo: Ascend the Arzviller boat lift again
Swim off the boat in Étang de Gondrexange
Photograph Xouaxange's old stone canal bridge
Fish the reed-lined ponds at dusk
5.mooringTip: Moorings thin out west of Niderviller, so plan your stop rather than assume a quay. There are rural pontoons near Gondrexange and quiet piled banks around Xouaxange; the lakes shallow off at the edges, so keep to the marked channel.

6.shortDescription: Deeper into rural Lorraine, the day finishes at Lagarde's busy pleasure port, with a chandlery for provisions and a canal-side kitchen at PK 209.
6.description: The canal keeps its rural mood all the way to Lagarde. You pass the shoulder of the Gondrexange lakes, work through a run of locks near Héming, and roll on into the Lorraine nature park, herons lifting off the water ahead of the bow. It is easy cruising with time to talk. Lagarde is a proper waterway hub. The pleasure port carries around forty berths with diesel, water, showers and a chandlery-cum-grocery, which makes it the sensible place to restock before the last day. Right on the bank at kilometre point 209, the Restaurant PK 209 plates up Lorraine cooking and freshwater fish, with a terrace over the water for a warm evening. Book a table in high season; the port fills with hire crews doing the same loop.
thingsToDo: Restock at the Lagarde port chandlery
Eat Lorraine fish on the PK 209 terrace
Watch herons over the nature park reaches
Top up fuel and water before the final leg
6.mooringTip: Lagarde is a full-service base with roughly forty berths, so book ahead in July and August when returning hire fleets crowd the pontoons. Fuel, water and pump-out are on site, making this the last easy top-up before Hesse.

7.shortDescription: You close the loop east to the Hesse base at an easy pace, with time for a last swim and a detour to Sarrebourg's Chagall window.
7.description: The loop closes gently. Turning back east, you cover familiar water through Gondrexange and Héming, but with the boat now in your hands it reads as calm rather than new. Leave enough margin for the locks; VNF crews knock off for lunch, and you want the boat cleaned and fuelled before hand-back time, not after. There is usually room for one more pause. A last swim in the lakes, or a short taxi from the canal into Sarrebourg to stand under Marc Chagall's blue stained-glass wall in the Chapelle des Cordeliers, rounds off the week nicely. Then it is the short final stretch into Hesse, past the abbey church, and back onto the home pontoon. Coil the ropes, and let the crew argue about which day was the best.
thingsToDo: See Chagall's stained glass in Sarrebourg
Take a final swim in the Lorraine lakes
Pass Hesse's Benedictine abbey church by boat
Refuel and clean up before hand-back
7.mooringTip: Time your last locks around the midday break so you reach Hesse with a comfortable margin before the agreed return. Bases usually want the boat back by a set morning hour, so an overnight at Hesse the night before beats a dawn dash.

brittany-islands-14-days.metaDesc: Two weeks from Le Crouesty through the Gulf of Morbihan to Houat, Belle-Ile, Groix and Lorient. Anchorages, harbours and mooring tips for South Brittany.

1.shortDescription: Catch the flood up the narrows into the Gulf of Morbihan and lock into the marina at the heart of old Vannes.
1.description: The first leg is short but it runs on the clock, not the log. Time your departure so the flood carries you through the gap between Port-Navalo and Kerpenhir, where the Jument current runs hard on big coefficients. Once inside, the water calms and the channel to Vannes winds past oyster trestles and low green shores. The marina sits right in the old town, reached through a swing bridge and sill that only open around high water, so check the timetable before you commit. Ashore, the ramparts and the wash-houses along the Marle are worth an hour, and the lanes behind the cathedral are full of creperies. We usually arrive with time to walk the walls before dinner. Buy your bread and coffee here; it is the best-stocked stop of the whole loop.
thingsToDo: Walk the medieval ramparts above the wash-houses
Creperie dinner in the old town lanes
Cathedral of Saint-Pierre and Place Henri IV
Provision fully at the city-centre market
1.mooringTip: The swing bridge and sill into Vannes basin open only for a window around high water, so plan your arrival and departure to the published timetable. Berths are in the heart of town and fill in July, so call the capitainerie ahead.

2.shortDescription: A lazy morning in Vannes, then a short drift down the Gulf to the green lanes and mooring field of Ile aux Moines.
2.description: Leave Vannes on the ebb and let the current do the work down to Ile aux Moines, the largest of the Gulf islands and its prettiest. The passage is only a few miles, but the tide sets hard through the narrows off Port-Blanc, so line up your timing and keep an eye on the transit. Pick up a buoy off Port du Lerio; the holding is patchy and the fairway busy, so a mooring beats the anchor here. Rent a bike from the quay and ride out to the Bois d'Amour and the megalith at the Pointe du Trech. The village keeps a couple of good spots for a plateau of oysters. Evenings are quiet once the day-trippers catch the last shuttle back to Port-Blanc.
thingsToDo: Cycle the Bois d'Amour woodland path
Oyster platter at a Le Bourg terrace
Megalith and view at Pointe du Trech
Swim off Plage du Goret
2.mooringTip: Take a visitor buoy off Port du Lerio rather than anchoring; the fairway is narrow and the bottom uneven. Tidal streams run strongly past the island, so approach near slack water if you can.

3.shortDescription: Ride the ebb out of the Gulf and reach across Quiberon Bay to France's most famous racing harbour on the River Crac'h.
3.description: This is the day you leave the sheltered water behind. Slip out through the Port-Navalo narrows on the last of the ebb, then set a course west across Quiberon Bay, usually a fine reach in the afternoon sea breeze. The entrance to the River Crac'h is buoyed and straightforward, and the marina lies about a mile upstream past ranks of oyster barges under the Kerisper bridge. La Trinite is serious sailing country; Tabarly kept his boats here and the Spi Ouest-France still fills the pontoons every spring. Walk the quay to read the transom names on the racing hulls. For dinner, La Maison Quintin does oysters straight off the river, and Le Chantier serves plain grilled fish from an old oyster shed across the water.
thingsToDo: Read the racing transoms along the pontoons
Oysters at La Maison Quintin terrace
Grilled fish at Le Chantier
Walk over the Pont de Kerisper
3.mooringTip: La Trinite is a large, well-run marina with a visitors' pontoon near the fuel berth; call ahead in regatta season when the Crac'h fills fast. The river current runs briskly, so approach your berth stemming the tide.

4.shortDescription: Sail the open bay to car-free Houat and drop the hook off the long white dune arc of Treac'h er Goured.
4.description: Houat sits out in the open bay, and the passage from La Trinite gives you a proper sail across ten miles or so of Quiberon water. The island is small, treeless and car-free, ringed by sand. On the east side, Treac'h er Goured curves for two kilometres of white dune, and in settled westerlies it makes a glorious day anchorage. Anchor off the beach clear of the marked cable zone, or pick up a buoy at Port Saint-Gildas on the north coast. The tiny village is a ten-minute walk up from the harbour, with a bakery and one or two places for crab. In a north-easterly the beach anchorage rolls at night, so shift round to the more sheltered water off Treac'h er Salus.
thingsToDo: Walk the dune arc of Treac'h er Goured
Crab and langoustine in the village
Coast path around Pointe de Beg Pell
Swim off the white eastern beaches
4.mooringTip: The Port Saint-Gildas buoy field has 25 to 30 visitor moorings and fills early in August, so arrive by mid-afternoon. For a settled night, anchor off Treac'h er Salus, which shelters better from the overnight north-easterly breeze.

5.shortDescription: A short hop south to Houat's wilder little sister, Hoedic, all heath, forts and empty beaches.
5.description: Hoedic lies only a few miles south of Houat, so this is a morning of easy sailing and an afternoon ashore. The island is even smaller and quieter than its neighbour, a low carpet of heath and dune with a handful of houses at the centre. Pick up a buoy in Port de l'Argol, the little harbour on the west side, or anchor off in calm weather. There are no cars and barely a shop, which is rather the point. Walk out to the old Fort d'Hoedic and the chapel, then down to the Plage de la Vierge for a swim. Evenings here are about as still as sailing in France gets. Bring what you need from Houat; provisioning on Hoedic is thin.
thingsToDo: Explore the restored Fort d'Hoedic
Swim at the Plage de la Vierge
Walk the heath to the lighthouse
Sundowner on deck in total quiet
5.mooringTip: Port de l'Argol offers visitor buoys but little shelter in strong westerlies, so check the forecast before committing to a night. Provisions are minimal, so top up food and water at Houat first.

6.shortDescription: Cross to Belle-Ile, the biggest of the Breton islands, and berth beneath the Vauban citadel guarding busy Le Palais.
6.description: Belle-Ile earns its name and its reputation, and the crossing from Hoedic runs about fifteen miles across open water, so pick a settled window. Le Palais is the island's front door, a working harbour under the great star-shaped Citadelle Vauban that Louis XIV's engineer built to hold the approaches. It gets crowded in season; expect to raft three or four deep on the outer pontoons, or lock into the inner basin when the gate opens near high water. Once tied up, the town has everything after days on the outer islands: chandlery, markets, a cinema, and proper restaurants. Tour the citadel ramparts for the view back over the masts. Hire bikes or a car here if you plan to see the wild south coast and the Aiguilles de Port-Coton.
thingsToDo: Tour the star-shaped Citadelle Vauban
Bike to the Aiguilles de Port-Coton
Market shopping on the quay
Cinema or a proper restaurant dinner
6.mooringTip: Le Palais fills fast in July and August, so arrive before midday and expect to raft several boats deep on the visitor pontoons. The inner basin gate opens only around high water, so time it or take an outer berth.

7.shortDescription: Just a few miles round the coast to Sauzon, Belle-Ile's prettiest port, all pastel quays and a drying tidal creek.
7.description: You could almost walk it, but sailing round from Le Palais to Sauzon is the nicer way to arrive. Sauzon sits at the head of a narrow tidal inlet on the north-west coast, a curve of pastel houses and a slim lighthouse at the entrance. Most of the harbour dries, so visitors pick up fore-and-aft moorings on the trots in the channel; the deeper berths at the outer end stay afloat. It is calmer and more genteel than Le Palais, made for a slow afternoon. Walk the coast path out to the Pointe du Cardinal, then come back for dinner. The Hotel du Phare on the quay has a terrace over the water and does the day's catch simply and well.
thingsToDo: Coast path to the Pointe du Cardinal
Dinner on the Hotel du Phare terrace
Photograph the pastel quayside houses
Walk out to the harbour lighthouse
7.mooringTip: Most of Sauzon dries, so take a fore-and-aft trot mooring in the channel and check you have the depth over low water for your draft. The outer moorings stay afloat; ask the harbourmaster where to lie.

8.shortDescription: A longer open-water leg north-west to Ile de Groix and its old tuna harbour of Port Tudy.
8.description: This is the longest single hop of the trip, roughly twenty miles of open Atlantic from Belle-Ile up to Groix, so leave early and watch the forecast for anything in the west. Groix rewards the effort. Port Tudy, on the north coast, was once one of France's great tuna ports, and the stone quays and old canning sheds still frame the basin. The marina is popular and takes no reservations, so you raft up where the harbourmaster points you. Ashore, walk or cycle out to the Plage des Grands Sables, a rare convex beach that shifts shape with the currents. The island keeps a tuna festival in its bones and a good line in seafood. We like the quiet of Port-Lay, the tiny cove just west of the main harbour.
thingsToDo: Cycle to the convex Plage des Grands Sables
Wander the old canning sheds at Port Tudy
Sunset walk to tiny Port-Lay
Seafood on the Port Tudy quay
8.mooringTip: Port Tudy takes no bookings and rafts boats deep in season, so arrive by early afternoon and be ready to lie alongside another yacht. The outer trots roll in northerly swell; the inner basin is calmer if there is room.

9.shortDescription: A short sail east into the great bay of Lorient, France's sailing city, past the concrete submarine pens.
9.description: Groix to Lorient is a gentle seven miles, so there is no rush off the mooring. The entrance to the bay is wide and well marked, but the ferries and fishing boats keep you alert, and the ebb can run hard in the channel. Lorient is where French offshore racing lives. The old German submarine base at Keroman now holds the Cite de la Voile Eric Tabarly and a pontoon full of IMOCA and Ultim campaign boats; you can often watch a crew working on a foiling giant. The city-centre marina is handy for provisioning and a night off the islands. It is not pretty in the postcard sense, but for anyone who follows the Vendee Globe it is close to a pilgrimage. Restock the boat here before turning south again.
thingsToDo: Cite de la Voile Eric Tabarly museum
See the IMOCA and Ultim racing pontoons
Tour the Keroman submarine base
Full provisioning run in the city
9.mooringTip: Lorient has several marinas; the city-centre pontoons are the most convenient for shops and the train. Watch the strong ebb and the commercial and ferry traffic on the way in, and call ahead in race weeks when berths vanish.

10.shortDescription: Back out to Groix for a beach day, anchoring off the pale sands rather than squeezing into Port Tudy.
10.description: Turn back out to Groix, but this time treat it as a day on the water rather than a harbour stop. In a westerly the anchorage off the Plage des Grands Sables on the east coast is hard to beat, a sweep of pale sand shelving into clear green shallows. Drop the hook off the beach and row ashore for a swim and a walk to Locmaria. If the wind swings into the east, tuck instead into the lee on the south side or go back to a buoy at Port Tudy. This is a good spare day to slow down, do a little maintenance, and let the crew off the leash. Time your evening move so you are settled before the sea breeze dies and the anchorage empties out.
thingsToDo: Anchor and swim off Plage des Grands Sables
Walk to the village of Locmaria
Snorkel the clear green shallows
Lazy lunch at anchor
10.mooringTip: In westerlies the Grands Sables anchorage is calm and sandy; in easterlies it becomes untenable, so move to the south coast or into Port Tudy. Set the anchor well, as the tide turns the boats through the day.

11.shortDescription: Retrace the open water south back to Belle-Ile, usually a fast downwind run to Le Palais.
11.description: The leg back to Belle-Ile covers the same twenty miles of open water, but in the prevailing westerly it usually runs the other way from day eight, downwind and quick. Poled out or under gennaker, you can eat the miles before lunch. Le Palais will feel familiar now, and a second night lets you see the parts of the island you missed. Rent bikes and ride the wild south coast to the Grand Phare and the needle rocks of Port-Coton, where the swell breaks white against the cliffs that Monet painted. If the harbour is full again, the anchorage off the citadel is fair in offshore winds. Provision for the run home; the outer islands ahead are thin on shops, and the Gulf is still two days away.
thingsToDo: Bike to the Grand Phare lighthouse
See the Aiguilles de Port-Coton cliffs
Second look around Le Palais old town
Provision for the outer islands
11.mooringTip: As on day six, Le Palais is busiest at midday; a mid-morning arrival off a fast downwind leg helps you find a raft space. In steady offshore wind the anchorage below the citadel is a quieter alternative.

12.shortDescription: Short reach back to Houat for a final island beach day before turning into the Gulf for home.
12.description: A short reach east brings you back to Houat, and with the outer legs behind you there is no reason to hurry. This is the trip's last real island day, so make it count. If the wind holds in the west, anchor off Treac'h er Goured again and spend the afternoon on that long white beach, or take the coast path right around the island in a couple of easy hours. The village bakery does a good far breton if you time it before the shelves empty. Grab a buoy at Port Saint-Gildas for the night, or lie off Treac'h er Salus if the breeze backs north. Watch the forecast closely now; tomorrow's run into the Gulf wants a reasonable window and a fair tide at the entrance.
thingsToDo: Last swim off Treac'h er Goured
Coast path loop around the island
Far breton from the village bakery
Sunset drinks at Port Saint-Gildas
12.mooringTip: Take a Port Saint-Gildas buoy for the night and settle early, as the field fills in high summer. If the wind backs into the north, the anchorage off Treac'h er Salus is the calmer choice for sleeping.

13.shortDescription: Cross Quiberon Bay back to home base at Le Crouesty, timing your arrival for the tidal gate off Port-Navalo.
13.description: The last full leg takes you back across Quiberon Bay to Le Crouesty, the big marina just outside the mouth of the Gulf. It is an easy sail in normal conditions, ten miles or so, often a beam reach in the sea breeze. Crouesty is a deep-water port that stays open on all tides, so there is no gate to catch on arrival, which makes it a relaxed place to end the sailing. Once berthed, walk round the headland to Port-Navalo for a last look at the Gulf entrance and the fierce current running through the narrows. The marina has fuel, water and a chandlery for anything you need to square away. If the boat is due back clean and full, this is the evening to start, not tomorrow morning.
thingsToDo: Walk the headland to Port-Navalo village
Watch the current race through the narrows
Refuel and top up water at Crouesty
Farewell seafood dinner at the marina
13.mooringTip: Le Crouesty is a large all-tide marina, so arrival timing is easy, but call the capitainerie for a visitor berth in peak weeks. Fuel up on arrival to avoid the morning queue on handover day.

14.shortDescription: A buffer day at Le Crouesty: a short local potter, a final swim, or simply pack up and hand back the boat.
14.description: The final day is deliberately loose. Most charters hand the boat back this morning, so it is really an insurance day against a blow that might have stranded you on the islands. If the weather has been kind and you are ahead, use it. A short potter up to Port-Navalo and a swim off the Gulf beaches makes a gentle last morning before you clear the cabins. Otherwise, this is time for the unglamorous end of a trip: strip the bunks, run the last of the provisions into a lunch, settle the fuel and the paperwork, and check nothing has crept under a floorboard. Two weeks around these islands leaves most crews already plotting the next loop. Le Crouesty makes an easy place to say goodbye to the boat.
thingsToDo: Short morning sail to Port-Navalo
Final swim off the Gulf beaches
Strip bunks and clear the cabins
Settle fuel, water and paperwork
14.mooringTip: Keep the boat on its Le Crouesty berth for the handover and confirm the check-out time with the base. If you take a short morning sail, be back well before the agreed return so the crew can inspect on schedule.

burgundy-saone-week.metaDesc: A relaxed 7-day river loop from Saint-Jean-de-Losne through Auxonne, Dole and Verdun-sur-le-Doubs, with easy locks, town quays and Burgundy cooking.

1.shortDescription: An easy upstream shakedown from France's biggest inland port to Auxonne, the garrison town where young Napoleon learned his gunnery.
1.description: Collect the boat at Saint-Jean-de-Losne, run through the handover, and point the bow upstream on the Grande Saône. It is a gentle first leg, roughly five hours against a lazy current, with big automated locks that do the work for you. The river runs wide and green here, past willows and the odd fisherman. Auxonne appears behind its Vauban ramparts. Napoleon trained as an artillery lieutenant in the barracks by the Place d'Armes, and the Château Louis XI now keeps a small museum to those years. Tie up at Port Royal or along the town quay, then walk the Porte de Comté and the tall Notre-Dame church before dinner. We usually leave the base by ten so there is time to settle in and still stroll the old streets before the light goes.
thingsToDo: Tour the Napoleon museum in Château Louis XI
Walk the Vauban ramparts and Porte de Comté
See the 13th-century Notre-Dame church on Place d'Armes
Browse the covered market for provisions
1.mooringTip: Port Royal has pontoon berths with water and power; the free town quay downstream fills up first in July and August, so aim to arrive before mid-afternoon.

2.shortDescription: Drop back down the Saône, turn up the Rhône–Rhin canal, and tie up below Dole's tall Notre-Dame tower in Louis Pasteur's home town.
2.description: This is the longest run of the week, so an early start pays off. Slip down the Saône to the hamlet of Saint-Symphorien, where the Canal du Rhône au Rhin peels off toward the Jura. From here the water narrows and the small locks begin, the canal weaving in and out of the Doubs through wooded banks. Dole arrives in the afternoon. Its port sits right below the collegiate church of Notre-Dame, a minute's walk from the Canal des Tanneurs and the cobbled tannery quarter where the old mills are now studios and bistros. Louis Pasteur was born on this waterside in 1822, and his childhood house still stands by the water. We tend to moor, shower, and head straight up into the old town for an apéritif before the restaurants fill.
thingsToDo: Visit Louis Pasteur's birthplace on the tanners' canal
Climb toward the Notre-Dame collegiate church tower
Wander the cobbled Canal des Tanneurs quarter
Try a Jura Savagnin wine at a bistro
2.mooringTip: Dole's hire-base pontoon and the town quay offer water, power and showers for a modest night fee. It is a popular stop in high season, so call the harbourmaster ahead or arrive before the locks close for the day.

3.shortDescription: Retrace the leafy Rhône–Rhin canal down to Saint-Symphorien, a quiet junction hamlet where the locks give way to open river.
3.description: A calmer day, and a pretty one. The canal back from Dole runs slow and shaded, herons lifting off the reeds as you pass, the Doubs glinting through the trees where the two channels braid together. The locks come at an easy rhythm, most of them worked by the boat's remote or the resident keeper. Saint-Symphorien itself is barely more than a church, a lock and a stone quay at the point where the Canal du Rhône au Rhin meets the Saône. That is the appeal. After a busy day in Dole, it is a spot to slow down, fish off the bank, and cook aboard. If the mood takes you, it is a short hop on to Saint-Jean-de-Losne for supplies, but there is no need to rush.
thingsToDo: Watch the Doubs and canal braid together
Fish or picnic from the stone quay
Photograph the village church at the junction
Cook aboard with market produce from Dole
3.mooringTip: Moor to the stone quay on the left bank just below the junction lock. Services here are basic, so top up water and shop in Dole or nearby Saint-Jean-de-Losne beforehand.

4.shortDescription: An open-water cruise down the Saône to Seurre, a former shipbuilding town with a secure marina steps from its main square.
4.description: Back on the broad Saône, the pace changes again. This stretch is proper river cruising: few locks, wide bends, and long views over the water meadows of the Bresse. Keep an eye out for herons and, if you are lucky, a kingfisher along the banks. Seurre sits on the right, its wooded islands and the old Mignot Bay basin sheltering the marina. The town built river barges for generations, and the quiet quays still carry that working-river feel. Step ashore into a tidy grid of streets, a couple of boulangeries, and the Écomusée that tells the Saône's story. We often break here mid-week to reprovision properly. It is an easy town to like, unhurried, with enough of a kitchen scene to make dinner ashore worthwhile.
thingsToDo: Visit the Écomusée in the old town
Stroll the Saône islands and river path
Restock at the boulangeries near the square
Watch for kingfishers along the banks
4.mooringTip: The marina sits in the sheltered Mignot Bay basin with a digicode gate, water and power, and only takes boats under about 18 metres. Book by phone in July and August, as the pontoons are close to town and popular.

5.shortDescription: Cruise on to Verdun-sur-le-Doubs at the meeting of two rivers, home of pôchouse, the local white-wine freshwater fish stew.
5.description: The turning point of the loop, and arguably the tastiest. A steady run down the Saône brings you to where the Doubs pours in from the east, and the little town of Verdun-sur-le-Doubs sits right on the join. The Port de la Confluence is friendly and well kept, with a brasserie, a wine bar and a launderette on the quay. What people really come for is pôchouse: a stew of river fish browned and simmered in Burgundy white, served over garlic-rubbed croutons, a recipe locals have guarded since the sixteenth century. Book a table and order it. Afterwards, walk the short heritage trail through the old streets and cross the footbridge to the Île du Château, an island of gardens in mid-river. It is a fine place to watch the day end.
thingsToDo: Order pôchouse at a quayside restaurant
Walk the 1.6 km heritage trail
Cross to the Île du Château gardens
Sit at the port's wine bar
5.mooringTip: Port de la Confluence lies about 500 metres up the Doubs from the actual junction; take the marked channel, not the main flow, and call ahead in summer for one of the 25 visitor berths.

6.shortDescription: Turn for home up the Saône, retracing quiet water back to Seurre for a second, more relaxed evening on familiar pontoons.
6.description: Homeward now, and no hurry about it. Cruising upstream on the Saône is slower than the run down, but the current here is mild and the locks are big and forgiving. Take the morning over it. This is the day to try the things you skipped: drop the boat's dinghy or a kayak in a quiet reach, swim off a shelving bank if the weather is warm, or simply sit up top with a coffee and watch the Bresse slide by. Seurre welcomes you back in the afternoon. Knowing the marina already, you can tie up quickly and give the evening to the town, a longer dinner, a walk along the islands, a nightcap on deck. Second nights in a place you liked are one of the underrated pleasures of a week afloat.
thingsToDo: Swim off a quiet shelving bank
Kayak a calm reach of the Saône
Return to a favourite Seurre restaurant
Walk the river islands at dusk
6.mooringTip: You already know the Mignot Bay pontoons, so aim to arrive by late afternoon and use the digicode gate. If the visitor berths are full, the harbourmaster can usually point you to a spare finger.

7.shortDescription: A short final leg up the Saône back to Saint-Jean-de-Losne, France's busiest inland port and the junction of two great waterways.
7.description: The last morning is a gentle one. It is a short upstream hop from Seurre to Saint-Jean-de-Losne, a couple of hours with a lock or two, so there is no rush to cast off at dawn. Ease back into the big marina where the week began, one of the largest inland ports in France and the point where the Canal de Bourgogne meets the Saône. There is life here: chandlery, workshops, barges of every vintage tied three deep. If your handover is not until morning, spend the last afternoon walking the quays, watching boats lock through, and finding a table at one of the waterside cafés for a final Burgundy glass. Rinse the freshwater off the deck, pack slowly, and let the trip settle. A loop like this ends where it started, but you arrive back a different sort of tired.
thingsToDo: Watch boats lock through at the junction
Browse the chandlery and river shops
Toast the trip at a waterside café
See the Burgundy Canal's first lock
7.mooringTip: Return berths at the H2O marina are assigned by the base, so radio or phone ahead as you approach. Give yourself time to reverse gently onto the pontoon, as the Saône's current runs past the entrance.

canal-du-midi-14-days.metaDesc: Two weeks on the Canal du Midi from Port Cassafieres: Fonseranes locks, Carcassonne, Castelnaudary cassoulet, Le Somail and the oysters of Marseillan.

1.shortDescription: A gentle shakedown leg west to Agde, where the round lock and a black basalt cathedral set the tone for the fortnight ahead.
1.description: Take the first day slowly. It is the leg where the crew learns which way the bow swings and how the boat sits under power. From Port Cassafieres the canal runs down toward Agde, a town cut from black volcanic basalt that gives it a stern, unusual look. The centrepiece here is the écluse ronde, a circular lock dating from the 1670s with gates opening three ways, toward Béziers, the Hérault river and the town itself. Lock keepers handle the mechanics, but keep fenders low and lines ready. Walk into the old town for the fortified Cathédrale Saint-Étienne, also basalt, its walls almost windowless. We usually provision properly here before heading upstream. There is a decent market and enough shops to fill the lockers for the days when villages thin out.
thingsToDo: See the 17th-century écluse ronde d'Agde
Visit the basalt Cathédrale Saint-Étienne
Provision at Agde's covered market
Walk the black-stone old town lanes
1.mooringTip: Agde's round lock is a highlight, not a hazard, but arrive before the 12:30 to 13:30 lunch closure or you will wait it out. Town-quay berths fill fast in July, so a bank mooring on stakes just short of Agde is quieter.

2.shortDescription: Retrace east and climb the Fonseranes staircase into Béziers, birthplace of the canal's engineer and home to a hilltop cathedral.
2.description: Today has the trip's signature climb. You backtrack past Port Cassafieres, cross the Orb on a long stone aqueduct, then face Fonseranes: eight linked oval chambers lifting boats more than twenty metres in a single flight. It is the busiest spot on the whole canal, so expect a queue and a crowd of onlookers on the banks. Take it in turns on the lines and let the eclusier set the pace. Béziers itself sits above the river, crowned by the Cathédrale Saint-Nazaire, whose terrace gives a long view over the vineyards. This was Pierre-Paul Riquet's home town, and there is a statue of him on the Allées Paul Riquet, a shaded avenue good for an evening stroll and a beer.
thingsToDo: Climb the eight-chamber Fonseranes lock flight
Cross the Pont-canal over the Orb
See Cathédrale Saint-Nazaire's hilltop terrace
Stroll the shaded Allées Paul Riquet
2.mooringTip: Moor at the basin below Fonseranes or on the pound above it, then walk or cycle the fifteen minutes into Béziers. The staircase only runs in set uphill and downhill sessions, so check the day's timetable at the lock hut rather than turning up and hoping.

3.shortDescription: Onto the lockless long pound, through the little Malpas tunnel to Capestang, where one of the canal's lowest bridges tests your air draft.
3.description: After the exertion of Fonseranes, this is a restful day with no locks at all. You join the grand bief, the fifty-four kilometre pound that Riquet held level across the plain. Not far along comes Malpas, the first canal tunnel ever dug, short and dim but only wide enough for one boat, so sound your horn and check nothing is coming the other way. Above it sits the Oppidum d'Ensérune, an Iron Age hilltop settlement worth the climb if you moor at Colombiers. Capestang closes the day, its huge Collégiale Saint-Étienne visible for miles over the vines. Mind the bridge on the approach, as it is among the lowest on the network and skippers with tall wheelhouses have been caught out here.
thingsToDo: Motor through the 161m Malpas tunnel
Climb to the Oppidum d'Ensérune
See the Collégiale Saint-Étienne de Capestang
Taste Minervois wine in the village
3.mooringTip: Capestang's port has around thirty berths with water, power and showers, handy after a dry run along the pound. Measure your air draft before the low road bridge on the way in; if in doubt, drop anything on the coachroof and cross slowly.

4.shortDescription: A slow amble along the pound to Le Somail, a one-bridge canal hamlet with a floating grocer and a barn of old books.
4.description: Still no locks, so there is no reason to rush. The pound winds on past Poilhes, a good spot for a late lunch on the water, and then reaches Le Somail. This is the village people remember most from a Midi trip. A humpbacked stone bridge from Riquet's era spans the cut, and beside it floats an old barge that still trades as a grocer. A few steps on you find Le Trouve Tout du Livre, a rambling shop with tens of thousands of second-hand books and prints stacked to the rafters. There is a 17th-century ice house nearby and a small chapel. The Canal de la Robine branches off toward Narbonne here, though that is a detour for another trip.
thingsToDo: Browse Le Trouve Tout du Livre bookshop
Buy supplies from the floating épicerie barge
Lunch on the water at Poilhes
See the old glacière ice house
4.mooringTip: Le Somail is small and popular, so the quay fills by mid-afternoon in season; arrive early or moor on the bank just outside the village and walk in. There is a mooring fee at the port itself, payable to the harbour office.

5.shortDescription: Past riverside wine cellars to Homps, an old barrel port on the edge of the Minervois where the locks start again.
5.description: The lockless run ends today. You pass Ventenac-en-Minervois, where a grand old cellar sits right on the bank and sells the local red, and then Paraza with its château above the water. At Argens-Minervois the pound finally gives way and the locks resume, so the pace slows and the crew earns its lunch again. Homps was once a serious wine port, the place where barrels of Minervois and Corbières were loaded for the long haul down to Bordeaux. Today it is a lively base with restaurants along the quay and a good spot to break the journey. We often stop early here, buy a few bottles from a nearby domaine, and eat aboard as the light goes soft over the water.
thingsToDo: Taste Minervois red at Ventenac's cellar
See Château de Paraza above the canal
Work the first Argens-Minervois lock
Dine on the quay at Homps
5.mooringTip: Homps is a hire base, so berths can be tight on Saturday changeover days; a weekday night is easier. Moor bows-in along the town quay for power and water, or find a quiet bank stretch just east of the village if you prefer stakes and silence.

6.shortDescription: A working day of locks through Minervois villages to Trèbes, ending at a triple staircase beside a good produce market.
6.description: This is a proper lock day, so set an early start and settle into a rhythm. The canal threads through La Redorte, Puichéric and Marseillette, each with its own chamber or two, and the crew soon has the drill down: fender, line, ease off, wait for the level. Vineyards run right to the towpath for much of it. Trèbes arrives at the end behind a three-chamber staircase, one of those spots where you handle three locks back to back and are glad of the break afterward. The village keeps a genuinely good line of local produce, wine, olives, confit and jam among it, so it is a fine place to restock. Waterside tables make an easy dinner once the boat is tied up.
thingsToDo: Work the three-chamber Trèbes staircase lock
Buy confit and olives in Trèbes
Pass the vineyards at Marseillette
Eat at a canal-side table in Trèbes
6.mooringTip: Time your approach to Trèbes so you clear the staircase before the 12:30 lunch break, or you will sit above it for an hour. The town quay has services and fills quickly, so a berth secured by early afternoon saves circling.

7.shortDescription: A short hop with a couple of locks into Carcassonne, whose floodlit medieval Cité is the set-piece of the whole route.
7.description: A gentle leg after yesterday's grind, with only a handful of locks to lift you toward Carcassonne. Tie up at the Port du Canal in the lower town, the Bastide Saint-Louis, which is a grid of 13th-century streets in its own right. The reason everyone comes, though, is above it: the Cité, a walled fortress of fifty-two towers restored by Viollet-le-Duc in the 1800s and now on the UNESCO list. It is a stiff twenty-minute walk up or a short bus ride from the port. Go late in the day when the coach crowds thin and the ramparts turn gold, then walk the outer walls after dark when the whole thing is lit. Book a table inside the walls for cassoulet if you can.
thingsToDo: Walk the ramparts of the medieval Cité
See Château Comtal inside the walls
Wander the Bastide Saint-Louis lower town
Eat cassoulet within the fortress walls
7.mooringTip: Carcassonne's Port du Canal is central but limited, so aim to arrive by early afternoon in high season. It is a walk or a quick bus up to the Cité; leave the boat and do not try to move it late, as the surrounding locks shut at 19:00.

8.shortDescription: Head upstream through quiet locks to Bram, a rare circular village wound in rings around its old church.
8.description: West of Carcassonne the canal grows quieter and the boats thin out. You climb steadily through a run of locks with long green stretches between them, the kind of day where the towpath cyclists become your main company. Bram is the stop, and it is unusual: the old village is a circulade, laid out in concentric rings around a former castle site, with a 13th-century church at the hub. Wander in from the port and the streets curl you around almost without meaning to. It is one of the oldest settlements in the Aude, and small enough to see in an hour. There is not much bustle here, which is rather the point after the crowds at the Cité.
thingsToDo: Walk Bram's concentric circulade streets
See the 13th-century village church
Cycle the shaded towpath west of Carcassonne
Picnic on a quiet lock lawn
8.mooringTip: Bram's mooring is small and calm, with a short walk into the village. If the port is full, the banks on the approach take stakes easily and the towpath here is firm; just keep clear of the lock cut so working boats can pass.

9.shortDescription: A lock-heavy climb to Castelnaudary, opening onto the canal's widest basin in the town that invented cassoulet.
9.description: Save your energy, because this short stretch is thick with locks, seventeen chambers packed into sixteen kilometres. It is slow, methodical work, and worth leaving by nine to stay ahead of other boats and the midday closure. The climb finishes at the Saint-Roch staircase, a four-chamber flight that lifts you into Castelnaudary. There the canal suddenly opens into the Grand Bassin, the widest expanse of water on the whole Midi, almost a small lake in the middle of town. Castelnaudary is the home of cassoulet, the slow-cooked bean and confit dish that turns up on every menu here. Order it properly, with a local red, and do not plan to move far afterward. This is the turning point of the fortnight.
thingsToDo: Climb the four-chamber Saint-Roch lock flight
Moor on the open Grand Bassin
Eat authentic cassoulet in town
Buy a cassole pot as a souvenir
9.mooringTip: The Grand Bassin gives plenty of room to moor, but it is open and can get choppy when the wind funnels across it, so take a berth on the sheltered town side. Clear the Saint-Roch staircase before the lunch closure; it is a bottleneck when several boats bunch up.

10.shortDescription: Turn for home and run the seventeen locks back down to Carcassonne, an easier descent but a long day at the ropes.
10.description: Now you turn the boat and start back east. The same seventeen locks you climbed yesterday come again, this time downhill, which is gentler on the engine but no shorter on the clock. Descending, you drop into each chamber and pay out line as the water falls, so keep hands clear of cleats under load. Leaving Castelnaudary early makes all the difference; the lunchtime shutdown at 12:30 can strand you between flights if you dawdle. Break the monotony at the villages along the way, and take on water where you can. By late afternoon Carcassonne's towers come back into view, and there is no shame in a second night below the Cité if the light is good and the ropes have earned it.
thingsToDo: Descend the seventeen locks toward Carcassonne
Top up water at a village tap
Photograph the Cité from the canal
Spend a second evening below the ramparts
10.mooringTip: Descending locks is easier on the crew but watch the lines as the boat drops; never make fast to a bollard on a falling level. If you reach Carcassonne late, the Port du Canal berths may be taken, so have a bank spot on the eastern approach in mind as backup.

11.shortDescription: Back east through Trèbes and the Minervois locks to Homps, retracing the prettiest wine country of the route.
11.description: The return run continues through country you already know, which is no bad thing on a canal, since the second pass shows you what the first missed. You drop through Trèbes and its staircase again, then work the chain of Minervois locks back toward Homps. Vineyards line most of the way, and by now the crew handles the ropes without thinking. It is a good day to slow the pace, stop at a village you skipped on the way up, and buy wine straight from a cellar door rather than a shop. Homps welcomes you back with its quayside tables. If the weather holds, eat outside and watch the hire boats jockey for berths as the afternoon winds down.
thingsToDo: Descend the Trèbes staircase eastbound
Buy wine at a Minervois cellar door
Revisit a village skipped on the climb
Dine quayside again at Homps
11.mooringTip: You know Homps by now, so aim for the same quay and grab a spot before the changeover rush if it is a Saturday. Bank moorings just outside town stay calmer overnight and put you a short walk from the restaurants.

12.shortDescription: Drop through the last Argens locks onto the long pound and glide, lock-free, past Le Somail to Capestang.
12.description: One more short bout of locks and then the easy water returns. You descend at Argens-Minervois and rejoin the grand bief, that long level pound where the boat simply runs and the crew can put the winch handles away. This is the day to enjoy the ride: coffee on the foredeck, a slow drift under the plane trees, a second look at Le Somail if you want to top up the book collection. The kilometres pass without effort as far as Capestang, back beneath its watchful collegiate church. After the lock-work of the past week, a lockless afternoon feels like a small holiday within the holiday. Keep an eye on that low bridge again on the way in.
thingsToDo: Descend the Argens-Minervois lock westbound
Drift the lockless grand bief under plane trees
Stop again at Le Somail's bookshop
Return to Capestang's shaded port
12.mooringTip: With no locks to time on the pound, you can arrive at Capestang later than usual, but the berths still go to early birds in summer. Re-check your air draft at the low bridge; nothing about it has changed since day three.

13.shortDescription: The big day: down Fonseranes, past Agde and out across the Étang de Thau to Marseillan's oysters and vermouth.
13.description: This is the longest push of the trip, so leave at first light. You run the last of the pound, then descend the Fonseranes staircase, cross back over the Orb and carry on past Port Cassafieres toward the sea. At Agde the round lock turns you toward the coast, and beyond it the canal ends at the Les Onglous lighthouse, where fresh water gives way to the wide Étang de Thau. The lagoon crossing is roughly eighteen kilometres of open water to Marseillan, and it wants respect: follow the marked channel and cross in the morning calm. Marseillan rewards the effort. It is the home of Noilly Prat vermouth, and the Thau around it is thick with oyster beds, best eaten fresh on the quay with a glass of cold Picpoul.
thingsToDo: Descend the full Fonseranes staircase
Pass the Les Onglous lighthouse onto Thau
Tour the Noilly Prat vermouth cellars
Eat Thau oysters with cold Picpoul
13.mooringTip: The Étang de Thau is a small inland sea and turns dangerous fast in a Tramontane over force four, so cross early and never at night. Stick to the buoyed channel, and book a berth at Marseillan port ahead in July, as the quay is popular and space is finite.

14.shortDescription: Cross the Thau back in the morning calm, lock through Agde one last time and return the boat to Port Cassafieres.
14.description: The last leg is short but demands the same care as yesterday. Recross the Étang de Thau early, while the water is still flat and the Tramontane has not woken, keeping to the channel markers back toward the Les Onglous light. Once inside the canal the pressure comes off. You lock through the round lock at Agde a final time, now old hands at it, and follow the familiar cut back to Port Cassafieres. Leave enough of the morning to clean up, empty the lockers and hand the boat back without a rush; most bases want it returned by a set time. It is worth a last coffee on deck to take stock. Two weeks and the better part of a hundred locks lie in your wake.
thingsToDo: Recross the Étang de Thau at dawn
Lock through the Agde round lock
Clean and hand back the boat
Toast the fortnight on deck
14.mooringTip: Time the Thau crossing for the early calm, then aim to reach Port Cassafieres with a comfortable margin before your handback slot. Top up fuel and water near Agde if the contract requires a full tank, and leave the mooring lines coiled ready for the next crew.

canal-du-midi-classic.metaDesc: Seven days from Castelnaudary to Beziers on the Canal du Midi: Carcassonne's walls, Minervois wine ports, the Malpas tunnel and Fonseranes locks.

1.shortDescription: Ease out of Castelnaudary's Grand Bassin and work the lock ladder down to Bram, cassoulet country slipping behind you.
1.description: The Grand Bassin at Castelnaudary is the widest stretch of the whole canal, so you get room to find your feet before the first bollard. Take on the cassoulet the town is famous for at lunch, then cast off. The run to Bram threads a string of lock chambers, and boats bunch up here as everyone learns the ropes, so a calm hand on the throttle pays off. Remember the ecluses shut between roughly 12:30 and 13:30, and again by seven in the evening. Bram itself is a quiet, circular village you can walk in twenty minutes. We usually tie up early, take on water at the base, and stroll the ring of old lanes before supper.
thingsToDo: Lunch on Castelnaudary cassoulet before casting off
Practise lock handling on the Bram ladder
Walk Bram's circular medieval lanes
Take on water at the Castel Nautique base
1.mooringTip: Bram's quay has water and power but limited berths, so aim to arrive before mid-afternoon. Bank mooring with stakes works well on the shaded reaches if the quay is full.

2.shortDescription: A steady day east to Carcassonne, where the port sits below the turreted Cite, one of Europe's great walled towns.
2.description: Carcassonne earns its own day, and most crews give it two nights. The town moorings sit in a basin just below the lower town, an easy walk or short bus ride from the medieval Cite on the hill. Cross the old bridge, climb to the double ring of ramparts, and you can spend hours on the wall walk above the vineyards. Go early or late; the ramparts fill with day-trippers by mid-morning in season. Back at the port, the quay has power and water, and there are enough cafes in the Bastide Saint-Louis for a proper dinner ashore. The approach is straightforward, but the basin gets busy, so scout your spot on the way in.
thingsToDo: Walk the ramparts of the medieval Cite
Cross the Pont Vieux into the old town
Dinner in the Bastide Saint-Louis
Refuel and top up water at the port
2.mooringTip: The Carcassonne basin has serviced quays but fills fast in July and August. Arrive by early afternoon, or moor along the bank just west and walk in.

3.shortDescription: Just a couple of hours to Trebes, home to a triple lock and a mill-turned-restaurant sitting right over the water.
3.description: This is a short hop, which suits a slow morning. Trebes announces itself with a staircase of three lock chambers, always good theatre, and there is usually a lock-keeper's stall selling honey or wine beside the gates. Moor along the village quay and the day is yours. Le Moulin de Trebes, an old watermill on the bank at the three locks, does a weekday lunch menu for around twenty-three euros with the terrace looking straight onto the passing boats. The church of Saint-Etienne is worth a look for its painted wooden corbels. With so little distance to cover, we often push a couple of extra kilometres past the village and moor in open country for a quieter night.
thingsToDo: Watch boats climb the Trebes triple lock
Lunch on the terrace at Le Moulin de Trebes
See the painted corbels in Saint-Etienne church
Buy honey or wine at the lock stall
3.mooringTip: The Trebes quay is popular and shallow at the very edge, so nose in gently and keep fenders low. Book Le Moulin de Trebes ahead in summer, especially for evening service.

4.shortDescription: Into the Minervois: locks and vineyards to Homps, once a busy port shipping barrels of red down toward Bordeaux.
4.description: Today the canal leaves the wide plain and starts to feel like wine country. You pass through Puicheric and La Redorte, the latter grouped around a chateau that is now a hotel with a good table if you fancy a stop. The reaches here are pretty and well shaded where the plane trees still stand. Homps was one of the canal's hardest-working ports, loading Minervois and Corbieres wine for the long haul toward Bordeaux, and the old warehouses still line the quay. It makes a natural overnight. Tie up, walk the village, and find a cellar for a tasting; the Minervois reds are honest and cheap at source. Locks are frequent through here, so keep an eye on the lunchtime closure.
thingsToDo: Taste Minervois reds at a Homps cellar
Pause at the chateau hotel in La Redorte
Walk Homps' old wine-trading quay
Work the locks through Puicheric
4.mooringTip: Homps has a serviced basin plus free bank mooring either side; the basin is handier for water and power. Approach slowly, as the old wine-port quay silts near the edge.

5.shortDescription: Vineyard villages the whole way to Le Somail, a hamlet barely big enough for its stone bridge, chapel and cellar-turned-bookshop.
5.description: A lovely stretch, and largely lock-free once you clear Argens. Argens-Minervois sits under a fourteenth-century chateau with cobbled lanes worth a wander, and the canal then rolls past Roubia, Paraza and Ventenac, each with its wine cave close to the water. Look out for the little aqueduct at Repudre, one of the oldest on the whole system. Le Somail is the prize at the end. It is barely a hamlet: a humpback stone bridge, a chapel, an ice-cream boat, and Le Trouve Tout du Livre, a former wine cellar packed with fifty thousand secondhand books. Moor along the bank near the bridge and stay for the evening light. It goes quiet once the day boats leave, which is exactly the point.
thingsToDo: Browse 50,000 books at Le Trouve Tout du Livre
Wander the chateau lanes of Argens-Minervois
Taste wine at the Ventenac-en-Minervois cave
Photograph Le Somail's humpback bridge
5.mooringTip: Le Somail has no locks to hold you up, so arrive by early afternoon; the bankside spots near the bridge go first. Stakes hold well in the towpath here.

6.shortDescription: On to Capestang beneath its dominating Gothic church, threading what is often called the lowest bridge on the whole canal.
6.description: There is a junction near Le Somail where the Canal de Jonction branches off toward the Robine and Narbonne; ignore it and stay on the main line for Capestang. The village skyline is all church: the collegiale Saint-Etienne, a great unfinished Gothic pile raised by the same masons who built Narbonne cathedral, with a bell-tower view over the vineyards for anyone who climbs it. Watch the road bridge on the way in, long known as the lowest arch on the canal; if your air draft is marginal, keep to the centre and go dead slow. Moor at the village port, which has water and power, and walk up through the old streets to the church. There is a village market some mornings if your timing is lucky.
thingsToDo: Climb the collegiale Saint-Etienne bell tower
Take in the openwork Gothic choir
Wander Capestang's old trading port and streets
Browse the village market if it's running
6.mooringTip: The Capestang road bridge is famously low, so if your boat's air draft is tight, check the gauge and pass slowly down the middle. The port quay has services but limited length.

7.shortDescription: A grand finish: the Malpas tunnel bored through rock, then the great Fonseranes lock staircase dropping down into Beziers.
7.description: The last day packs in the canal's headline acts. First comes the Malpas tunnel, a hundred and sixty-five metres bored straight through the hill in 1679, the first navigable tunnel ever cut for a canal. It runs one boat at a time, so wait your turn and sound off before you enter. Just above it, a short walk up, sits the Oppidum d'Enserune with a wide view over an old drained lagoon. Then the ground drops away at Fonseranes, a staircase of linked lock chambers that lowers boats toward the Orb. Most crews moor above the flight for the night and walk down to watch, then head into Beziers for Saint-Nazaire cathedral on its bluff. The Cers wind can gust hard on this open eastern end, so tie on well.
thingsToDo: Pass through the historic Malpas tunnel
Climb to the Oppidum d'Enserune viewpoint
Watch boats descend the Fonseranes staircase
Visit Saint-Nazaire cathedral above Beziers
7.mooringTip: Moor above the Fonseranes flight for the night rather than locking down late, since the staircase is manned and keeps set hours. Beziers' basin can be exposed to the Cers, so double up your lines.

cannes-lerins-st-tropez.metaDesc: A 7-day yacht route from Cannes through the Lérins, Saint-Tropez, Cavalaire, Porquerolles and Port-Cros, back along the red Esterel coast.

1.shortDescription: A short hop from the marina to two pine-covered islands, one guarding a famous prison cell, the other still farmed by monks.
1.description: Barely an hour out of Cannes and you drop the engine off Sainte-Marguerite, the larger of the two Lérins. The channel between it and Saint-Honorat is one of the calmest spots on this coast, ringed by Aleppo pines and eelgrass you must not anchor over. Pick the sandy patches instead, and give the marked protection zones a wide berth. Ashore on Sainte-Marguerite, the old Fort Royal held the man in the iron mask; the walk up through the pines takes twenty minutes. Saint-Honorat is quieter, still worked by Cistercian monks who press their own wine and the Lérina liqueur. We usually run this leg late morning, swim off the boat through the afternoon, and stay put for a still first night.
thingsToDo: Walk up to Fort Royal, Sainte-Marguerite
Taste Lérina liqueur at Saint-Honorat abbey
Swim the channel between the two islands
Follow the shaded pine paths of Sainte-Marguerite
1.mooringTip: Anchor only on the sand patches in the channel; the eelgrass beds are protected and holding is poor over weed. It gets busy by noon in July, so arrive early or take the outer edge of the field.

2.shortDescription: A proper passage across the bay to the most photographed harbour on the coast, with a market square worth the early start.
2.description: This is the first real sail of the week, a little over twenty miles of open water with Cap Camarat closing the gulf ahead. Leave by nine and you will be tied up before the afternoon breeze fills in. Saint-Tropez wants booking well ahead in season; the harbourmaster puts you stern-to along the quay, close enough to read the menus. Café Sénéquier and its red terrace have watched the port since 1887. Behind the Quai Jean-Jaurès the Ponche quarter keeps its pastel walls and narrow lanes, quiet once the day boats leave. Time it for a Tuesday or Saturday and the Place des Lices fills with market stalls from first light, tapenade and Provençal cheese and far too many hats.
thingsToDo: Coffee on Sénéquier's red terrace
Wander the Ponche old quarter's lanes
Shop the Place des Lices market
Climb to the Citadelle for the view
2.mooringTip: Book a berth in Saint-Tropez well in advance for July and August; the quay is stern-to and fills fast. If it is full, anchor off the north of the gulf toward Les Canoubiers and tender in.

3.shortDescription: Ten miles around two wild capes drop you into a sheltered bay of sand, with a wine village and a coastal reserve close behind the beach.
3.description: Only ten miles today, but they round some of the best coast in the gulf. Off Cap Taillat and Cap Lardier the land belongs to the Conservatoire du Littoral, steep green inlets with no road behind them, worth dropping the hook for a lunch swim if the sea is calm. Cavalaire itself sits in a broad sandy bay that shrugs off the Mistral but opens to anything from the east and southwest, so read the forecast before you commit to a night. Free anchoring is banned in the bay; take one of the moorings or a visitor berth in the port. The town is unfussy and geared to sailors, with easy provisioning and a string of Côtes de Provence growers up the valley behind it.
thingsToDo: Swim the wild inlets off Cap Lardier
Provision at Cavalaire's morning market
Taste Côtes de Provence rosé inland
Walk the sandy sweep of Cavalaire beach
3.mooringTip: Anchoring is prohibited across Cavalaire bay, so pick up a mooring buoy or book a visitor berth at the port. The holding on mud and weed is fine, but the bay is untenable in easterlies, so move on if the wind backs.

4.shortDescription: A solid half-day west to Porquerolles, largest of the Golden Isles, finishing off Plage Notre-Dame, one of the Med's finest white beaches.
4.description: Around twenty-two miles west, past Cap Bénat, and the Îles d'Or come up out of the haze. Porquerolles is the largest and the greenest, all vineyards, pine and eucalyptus, and it sits inside a national park with real teeth. You need a black-water holding tank to anchor legally here. The buoy field off the port takes boats up to thirteen metres and books out in July, so reserve ahead or plan to anchor well off the sand. Plage Notre-Dame, a long walk or cycle east of the village, is the one people come for, pale sand under umbrella pines. The village itself gathers round the Place d'Armes, a dusty square with boules, a couple of bars and bikes for hire by the hour.
thingsToDo: Cycle to Plage Notre-Dame beach
