Play boules on the Place d'Armes
Ride up to Fort Sainte-Agathe
Taste island wine from Porquerolles vineyards
4.mooringTip: Reserve a harbour buoy in advance; the field is limited to boats under thirteen metres and fills through the summer. To anchor you need a black-water tank aboard and must stay clear of the marked Posidonia beds, at least 300 metres off the beaches.

5.shortDescription: Eight miles gets you to the wildest of the three islands, all forest and clear water inside Europe's first marine national park.
5.description: Under eight miles island to island, so there's no rush off the mooring. Port-Cros is the smallest of the Golden Isles and the least touched, a hump of dense forest that runs down to the water with almost no building on it. The main road, so to speak, is a footpath. The rade in front of the harbour holds boats on buoys and along the quay, protected from most directions except a hard southerly. Over twelve metres and you must take a buoy in the Bagaud zone; anchoring between Bagaud and the port is now banned outright. Off La Palud on the north side, a marked snorkel trail runs over six buoys, and on a good day you will drift over grouper and barracuda. Save an hour for the walk up to Fort de l'Estissac.
thingsToDo: Snorkel the marked trail off La Palud
Walk up to Fort de l'Estissac
Anchor quietly in Port-Man bay
Follow the forest path across the island
5.mooringTip: Take a buoy in the Rade de Port-Cros; anything over twelve metres is required to, and anchoring between Bagaud and the harbour is prohibited. Book where you can, arrive by early afternoon, and remember a black-water tank is mandatory in the park.

6.shortDescription: The big passage back east, a long day under sail to where the red Esterel rock drops straight into the sea.
6.description: This is the long one. Reckon on forty-odd miles back east, so we slip the buoy at first light and settle in for a full day on the water. The reward comes at the end, when the Esterel massif rises ahead in fired-up red, volcanic rock falling sheer into deep blue. If the timing works, drop into Cap Roux on the way in, sandy bottom in five to eight metres and good shelter from the west. Île d'Or, with its little Saracen tower, is private, so you round it rather than land. Théoule sits at the western corner of the Bay of Napoule, tucked out of the northwest and holding a handful of restaurants and a beach or two, well clear of the Cannes crush across the water.
thingsToDo: Anchor under the red cliffs of Cap Roux
Round Île d'Or and its Saracen tower
Swim the Esterel coves near Anthéor
Dine on the quiet Théoule waterfront
6.mooringTip: Start this leg at dawn to beat the afternoon breeze on a long passage. Théoule anchorage in the Bay of Napoule shelters you from northwest through south, but for a settled night the port or a Cannes-side berth is the safer bet.

7.shortDescription: An easy final morning across the bay to Cannes, time for one last swim before you hand the boat back.
7.description: The shortest leg of the week, five miles or so across the Bay of Napoule with the Esterel at your back and Cannes ahead. No reason to hurry. If the charter base wants the boat by noon, there is still time to anchor for a last swim off Île Sainte-Marguerite on the way in, closing the loop where the week began. Coming into Cannes you pass under the old town, Le Suquet, its watchtower standing over the tiled roofs, and the long sweep of La Croisette with the palaces behind the beach. Get fenders and lines ready early; the marina at Cannes runs tight in season and the fuel dock can back up. Fuel, pump out, and step ashore for a proper lunch in the Suquet before you leave.
thingsToDo: Last swim off Île Sainte-Marguerite
Climb Le Suquet to the watchtower
Stroll La Croisette before departure
Lunch in the old town of Le Suquet
7.mooringTip: Coming into Cannes in season, have fenders and stern lines ready before you enter and call the marina ahead on VHF for a berth. The fuel dock backs up mid-morning, so top up early if you owe the base a full tank.

corsica-grand-tour-14-days.metaDesc: Two weeks from Ajaccio around Corsica: Scandola, Calvi, Bonifacio, the Lavezzi islands and the Gulf of Valinco, with real anchorage tips.

1.shortDescription: A gentle shakedown leg out of Napoleon's home port into the wide sandy bay of Sagone, quiet once the day boats leave.
1.description: Ajaccio is where most crews pick up the boat, so treat this first leg as a settling-in run rather than a race. Provision at the market near the old town before you slip lines, then point northwest out past the Sanguinaires islands guarding the gulf mouth. The bay at Sagone opens broad and sandy, ringed by low hills and a handful of beach eateries. By late afternoon the water-skiers and tripper boats thin out, and the anchorage turns calm enough for a first night at anchor. We usually aim to be in by five, giving the crew time to sort stowage and swim before dark. Holding is good over sand in three to five metres. Keep an eye west; the bay is open to swell if the wind backs, though summer evenings are typically settled here.
thingsToDo: Provision at Ajaccio's old-town covered market
Visit the Maison Bonaparte museum
Swim off Sagone's long sandy beach
Sundowner at a Sagone beach restaurant
1.mooringTip: Anchor off the beach at Sagone rather than crowding the small quay; the sand holds well in three to five metres. Set your hook by late afternoon before the evening breeze fills in.

2.shortDescription: Just up the coast sits Greek-founded Cargese, where a Catholic and an Orthodox church still face each other across the valley.
2.description: Only a handful of miles separate Sagone from Cargese, so there's no need for an early start. The village sits on a bluff above its little harbour, and its odd history shows in the two churches staring across the ravine at one another, one Latin Catholic, one Greek Orthodox, left over from Greek refugees who settled here in the seventeenth century. The harbour itself is small and can fill fast in July, so a lunchtime arrival helps. Below town the beaches run one after another. Plage de Pero and Plage de Chiuni are both worth the dinghy ride for an afternoon swim and a walk. Ashore, the cafes along the port square do a decent plate of charcuterie and a cold Pietra beer. We tend to anchor off Pero and leave the harbour to the ferries.
thingsToDo: Compare the Latin and Greek Orthodox churches
Swim at Plage de Pero or Chiuni
Charcuterie and Pietra beer on the port
Walk up to Cargese's old cemetery
2.mooringTip: The little harbour at Cargese is tight and often full by midday, so anchor off Plage de Pero to the north instead. Sand holding is reliable in four to six metres, open to the west.

3.shortDescription: A scenic run to the red porphyry headland of Capo Rosso, capped by its lone Genoese watchtower above the Gulf of Porto.
3.description: The coast north of Cargese turns dramatic. Capo Rosso rises in slabs of rust-red rock, with the Tour de Turghju perched some three hundred metres up on its spine, a stiff walk if you fancy the climb from a landing below. This is the doorway to the Gulf of Porto and the Calanche de Piana beyond, where wind and sea have carved the granite into spires. The cape is exposed, so read the forecast before committing to an overnight here. Many crews tuck into the sheltered coves inside the gulf instead, or press on to Ficajola with its tiny fishing beach. If the afternoon libeccio builds, don't linger off the point. We time the passage for morning calm and let the crew shoot photos while the light stays soft on the cliffs.
thingsToDo: Photograph the red cliffs of Capo Rosso
Climb to the Tour de Turghju watchtower
Dinghy into Anse de Ficajola beach
Detour into the Calanche de Piana rock spires
3.mooringTip: Capo Rosso itself is too exposed for a settled night, so use it as a daytime stop and anchor inside the Gulf of Porto or at Ficajola. Watch for afternoon westerlies funnelling round the point.

4.shortDescription: Past the Scandola reserve lies Girolata, a fort-topped hamlet you can reach only by boat or footpath.
4.description: Today you skirt the edge of the Scandola reserve, a UNESCO-listed jumble of red volcanic rock where ospreys nest on the cliffs and no landing is allowed. Keep offshore of the marked zone and enjoy it from the water. Girolata waits just south, hidden behind a low headland with a squat Genoese fort standing guard. The anchorage is one of the most sheltered on the island, ringed by dozens of fore-and-aft buoys laid in Cala Muretta and Cala Vecchia. Pick one up early; the field fills through the afternoon as tripper boats and yachts pour in. Ashore there's no road, just a cluster of restaurants serving the day's catch and grilled meats. We like to swim before the crowds arrive, then take the shore path for the view back down over the bay.
thingsToDo: Cruise the red cliffs of Scandola reserve
Pick up a buoy in Cala Muretta
Walk the coast path to the Genoese fort
Fresh grilled fish at a Girolata restaurant
4.mooringTip: Girolata's fore-and-aft buoys are the safe bet and hold you steady in any wind, but they go early. Aim to arrive before mid-afternoon, and expect to pay a fee to the boat that comes round collecting.

5.shortDescription: The longer northbound leg crosses open water to Calvi, where a Genoese citadel stands over one of Corsica's liveliest marinas.
5.description: This is the first proper passage of the trip, roughly twenty-five miles up to the Balagne coast, so a nine o'clock departure gives you comfortable margins. The coast is wild and roadless for much of the way, with few bolt-holes, which is why the forecast matters. Westerlies tend to build after noon, so an earlier start keeps the sea kinder. Calvi announces itself from far off, the citadel walls catching the light above a curve of pine-backed beach. The marina sits right under the ramparts and buzzes late into the evening. If it's full in high season, the anchorage in the Revellata bay just west is calm and pretty. Ashore, climb into the old town, where locals will tell you Columbus was born, and eat well along the quay. We book a berth ahead in August.
thingsToDo: Explore the Genoese citadel above Calvi
Swim off Calvi's pine-backed beach
Quayside dinner in the marina
Anchor overnight in Revellata bay
5.mooringTip: Calvi's marina fills fast in July and August, so radio ahead or arrive early for a berth under the citadel. If it's full, the Revellata anchorage a couple of miles west offers calm holding over sand.

6.shortDescription: The return south along the wild Scandola coast back to sheltered Girolata, with a possible quiet stop at Galeria.
6.description: Retracing your wake gives a different trip in reverse light, and the twenty-five miles back down to Girolata pass quickly with the usual afternoon breeze behind you. Break the run at Galeria if you fancy a change; the anchorage in its bay is quiet and sits just before the reserve boundary, handy for a lunch stop and a swim away from the crowds. From there it's a short hop back to Scandola's red buttresses, best seen with the sun swinging west. Girolata's buoy field will be filling again by the time you arrive, so don't dawdle over lunch. A second night here is no hardship; the little bay looks different in changing light, and by evening, once the day boats have gone, it settles into something close to silence. We grab an early buoy and swim off the stern.
thingsToDo: Lunch stop and swim at Galeria bay
See Scandola's red cliffs in evening light
Snorkel off the stern in Girolata
Evening drink ashore at the harbour
6.mooringTip: Coming back to Girolata a second time, you already know the drill: grab a fore-and-aft buoy early. Galeria makes a calm lunchtime anchorage on sand if you want to break the leg.

7.shortDescription: A long haul south past Sagone and the Sanguinaires islands, closing the first loop back in the Gulf of Ajaccio.
7.description: The longest single leg of the fortnight, this run tops forty miles, so an early departure isn't optional. Slip the buoy at first light while the sea is flat and the wind still sleeping. You'll pass the mouth of the Sagone gulf and round Capo di Feno before the Sanguinaires islands come up, four reddish islets marking the entrance to the Gulf of Ajaccio and worth a slow pass for the old semaphore and lighthouse. The gulf itself often kicks up a lumpy afternoon chop as the sea breeze funnels in, another reason to have the miles behind you by mid-afternoon. Ajaccio's marinas have space for visitors, and the town rewards a night off the anchor, with proper restaurants and a chance to top up stores. We reprovision here before the southern half begins.
thingsToDo: Slow pass by the Sanguinaires islands
Reprovision at Ajaccio's covered market
Dinner ashore in the old town
Aperitif along the Ajaccio marina quay
7.mooringTip: Ajaccio has two marinas, Tino Rossi by the old town and the larger Charles Ornano; call ahead in season. After a forty-mile day the visitor berths are worth the fee for showers and easy provisioning.

8.shortDescription: South around Capo di Muro into the Gulf of Valinco, dropping the hook off the sleepy sands of Porto Pollo.
8.description: With the western loop done, the boat turns south for the quieter half of the island. Clear the Gulf of Ajaccio and round Capo di Muro, a low rocky point that can raise a short sea when wind meets current, then bear into the broad Gulf of Valinco. Porto Pollo sits on the northern shore, a low-key village with a small beach, a scatter of eateries and clear water that begs a swim the moment the anchor bites. The holding is good over sand, and the bay is well protected from the prevailing westerlies. There's little to do ashore beyond eat, swim and slow down, which after the long haul from Girolata is rather the point. We drop the hook off the beach and let the crew loaf away the afternoon.
thingsToDo: Swim off Porto Pollo's sheltered beach
Snorkel the clear Valinco shallows
Seafood supper at a village eatery
Paddleboard along the northern shore
8.mooringTip: Anchor off the Porto Pollo beach in three to six metres over sand; holding is dependable and the bay shrugs off the usual westerly. Tuck in behind the point if a southerly is forecast.

9.shortDescription: Cross the gulf to Campomoro, where the largest Genoese tower in Corsica watches over a curved sandy beach.
9.description: The two shores of the Valinco sit barely a few miles apart, so it's a morning of easy sailing and an afternoon of doing very little. Campomoro curls around a neat sandy bay on the gulf's southern arm, crowned by the biggest Genoese tower on the island, a squat fortified block you can walk up to and look out from over the whole gulf. The village is tiny, just a few bars and restaurants strung behind the beach, which is exactly its charm. Anchor off the sand and swim ashore for a drink as the light goes gold on the tower. The bay is open to the west, so if the libeccio is up you'll roll a little at anchor. We pick a calm forecast for this one and enjoy the quiet.
thingsToDo: Walk up to the Campomoro Genoese tower
Swim from the curved sandy bay
Drink at a beachfront Campomoro bar
Snorkel the rocks below the tower
9.mooringTip: Anchor off Campomoro beach over sand, but note the bay is open west and rolls in a libeccio swell. A few seasonal buoys sit near the tower; otherwise pick a settled night here.

10.shortDescription: The long southbound run around Corsica's toe to Bonifacio, its old town clinging to white cliffs above a hidden harbour.
10.description: This is a committing leg, around twenty-five miles down to the island's southern tip, and the Strait of Bonifacio has a habit of accelerating whatever wind is about. Check the forecast hard before you leave, and if it's blowing hard from the west, sit tight another day. Rounding the last capes, Bonifacio hides its entrance well; you're almost on the cliffs before the narrow calanque opens to port and the marina reveals itself, tucked into a natural fjord below the ramparts. The medieval haute ville above is all limestone lanes and sea views, and the harbour restaurants run late. Berths are limited and demand is high, so book ahead in season or take a laid mooring further up the inlet. We time our arrival for early afternoon to be sure of a spot.
thingsToDo: Wander the limestone lanes of the haute ville
Climb the Escalier du Roi d'Aragon steps
Harbourside dinner in the calanque
View the cliffs from the marina entrance
10.mooringTip: Bonifacio's marina sits deep inside the calanque and is very busy in summer, so reserve a berth or take a laid mooring up the inlet. Enter under power; the fjord funnels gusts even when it looks calm.

11.shortDescription: Slip into the Bouches de Bonifacio reserve and moor off the granite boulders and clear coves of the Lavezzi islands.
11.description: The Lavezzi lie just six miles off Bonifacio, but in fully protected waters, so read the rules before you go. The islands lie inside the Bouches de Bonifacio reserve, the largest marine reserve in mainland France, and the coves are governed by numbered mooring fields laid since the 2022 regulations. Anchoring is restricted, and where it's allowed it's on sand only, never on the seagrass. Pick up a buoy in Cala di u Grecu or Cala Lazarina and you're swimming in some of the clearest water in the Med, over pale sand between rounded granite boulders. Ashore, a walking path leads to the cemetery marking the wreck of the frigate Semillante, lost here in 1855. The bays offer little shelter for the night, so most crews visit by day.
thingsToDo: Snorkel the granite coves of Cala Lazarina
Pick up a mooring in Cala di u Grecu
Walk to the Semillante shipwreck memorial
Swim over pale sand between the boulders
11.mooringTip: The Lavezzi coves use numbered ZMEL buoys under the 2022 reserve rules; grab one and never drop the hook on seagrass. The bays give little overnight shelter, so treat it as a daytime stop and sleep back at Bonifacio or Rondinara.

12.shortDescription: Rondinara waits up the east coast, a near-perfect horseshoe bay with good sand holding and summer buoys.
12.description: Turning north out of the strait, the character of the coast softens. Rondinara lies about ten miles up, between Bonifacio and Porto-Vecchio, and it's one of those bays that looks almost designed by hand, a rounded horseshoe of pale sand with a narrow mouth. That shape makes it a fine anchorage, well protected through the western half of the compass, from northerlies round to southerlies. Holding is good in sand, and a scatter of buoys appears in season if you'd rather not anchor. The beach draws day-trippers by road, so it can be busy at midday, but the crowd thins by evening and the bay comes into its own. We drop the hook toward the northern side, swim ashore, and stay put for the night. A calm, easy end to the southern loop.
thingsToDo: Swim in the horseshoe bay of Rondinara
Walk the sandy beach at dusk
Snorkel the rocky northern headland
Anchor for a quiet night aboard
12.mooringTip: Rondinara gives good sand holding and shelter from north through south on its western side; anchor toward the northern shore. Seasonal buoys appear in summer, and the bay is calmest once the beach crowd leaves in the evening.

13.shortDescription: The long leg back west around the southern capes to Propriano, the Valinco's busiest port and a good spot to restock.
13.description: The passage back to the west coast is a fair stretch, well over thirty miles, and it means rounding the exposed southern tip again, so an early start and a settled forecast are worth the discipline. Once past Capo di Feno and the Senetosa point, the coast eases you north into the familiar Gulf of Valinco. Propriano sits at its head, the busiest town on this stretch, with a working port that runs ferries to Sardinia and a waterfront lined with shops and restaurants. After a week of quiet anchorages the bustle is a welcome change, and the marina makes reprovisioning simple before the last hop home. We tie up, walk the front for an ice cream, and eat ashore. Fresh fish and a bottle of Corsican rose close the day nicely.
thingsToDo: Stroll Propriano's restaurant-lined waterfront
Restock at the town's shops and market
Fresh fish and Corsican rose ashore
Evening ice cream along the port
13.mooringTip: Propriano's marina takes visitors but is busy with the Sardinia ferry traffic, so call ahead and keep clear of the ferry berth on approach. Berthing here is easier than anchoring off the exposed town beach.

14.shortDescription: The final leg north around Capo di Muro and past the Sanguinaires, back to Ajaccio to hand over the boat.
14.description: The last day retraces the water between the two western gulfs, roughly thirty miles back up to Ajaccio. Leave in good time; charter handovers usually want the boat in by morning, and it's poor form to arrive flustered on the final approach. Round Capo di Muro one more time, then the Sanguinaires islands come up to mark the gulf entrance, a fitting last landmark with their reddish rock and old lighthouse. If the timing allows, a farewell swim in one of the coves along the way is a good way to sign off. Back in Ajaccio, refuel, fill the water tanks and start squaring away the boat before the handover. We like to be alongside by late afternoon, giving the crew time to pack unhurried and toast a fortnight well sailed.
thingsToDo: Farewell swim in a Sanguinaires cove
Round Capo di Muro for the last time
Refuel and water up at Ajaccio marina
Final crew dinner in the old town
14.mooringTip: Aim to be back in Ajaccio by late afternoon on the final day; charter bases want the boat cleared next morning. Refuel and top up water on the way in, and confirm your handover berth with the base by radio.

morbihan-quiberon.metaDesc: Seven days from Le Crouesty through the Gulf of Morbihan to Vannes, Houat, Belle-Île and Hoedic: car-free islands, tidal channels and Breton ports.

1.shortDescription: Slip out of Le Crouesty on the flood and ride the tide up into the gulf to car-free Ile aux Moines.
1.description:
The gulf entrance narrows off Port-Navalo, and the tide runs hard through it, so we usually leave Le Crouesty an hour or so after low water and let the flood carry us in. Two to three knots under the keel makes short work of the passage. Once inside, the water flattens and the islands open up around you.

Ile aux Moines sits near the middle of the gulf, its two visitor pontoons floating free of the shore. No cars run here. Hire a bike at the port and follow the lane south through the Bois d'Amour to Pointe de Brouël, where Ile d'Arz lies low across the water. The Dolmen des Pierres Plates rewards a short walk. Back at Port Blanc, a plate of oysters and a cold Muscadet closes the day well.
thingsToDo: Cycle the Bois d'Amour to Pointe de Brouël
Visit the Dolmen des Pierres Plates
Eat oysters back at Port Blanc
Walk the lane to Pointe du Trec'h
1.mooringTip: The two visitor pontoons sit detached from land, so arrive self-sufficient with your own water. Depths at the far end of the southern pontoon drop near 1.6m, so berth toward the inner end at low springs, or pick up a gulf mooring buoy off Port Blanc.

2.shortDescription: A short hop up the channel, timed to the tide, brings you through the lock into medieval Vannes and its walled old town.
2.description:
Vannes hides at the head of a narrow river, and you only get in for a couple of hours either side of high water. Plan backwards from that. We generally weigh anchor to arrive at the Kérino swing bridge on the rising tide, then motor gently up the canal past moored boats and reed banks. The lock holds the inner basin at just over two metres, so there is water even when the gulf outside has drained.

The reward is a berth in the centre of town. Vannes keeps its ramparts, its old washhouses along the Marle, and streets of leaning half-timbered houses. Cathédrale Saint-Pierre and the covered market at La Cohue are a few minutes' walk. Provision here; the market is good and the shops are close.
thingsToDo: Walk the ramparts and Marle washhouses
See Cathédrale Saint-Pierre in the old town
Browse La Cohue covered market
Provision at the Vannes town market
2.mooringTip: The inner basin opens only about 2.5 to 3 hours either side of high water, and you pass a swing bridge then a lock to reach it. Call the capitainerie ahead on VHF and time your run up the canal for the last of the flood.

3.shortDescription: Ride the ebb back down the gulf and out past Port-Navalo, then cross Quiberon Bay to the white sands of Houat.
3.description:
This is the longest day, so we like to be off the pontoon early and out of the gulf on the ebb. The channel past Port-Navalo runs fast when the tide is with you, and you sluice out into Quiberon Bay in no time. From there it is an open reach of a dozen or so miles across to Houat.

The island is a low ridge of granite and gorse with one small village. Boats gather in the Rade de Houat, the sandy road along the north side, or on the buoys off Saint-Gildas. The pull is Treac'h er Goured, a two-kilometre curve of white sand at the eastern end. Take the tender in and walk the dune. Note that anchoring off that beach is banned over the marked cable zone.
thingsToDo: Land the tender on Treac'h er Goured
Walk the two-kilometre dune arc
Wander Houat's single fishing village
Swim off the Rade sandbanks
3.mooringTip: Anchor in the Rade de Houat on the sand, well clear of the marked cable zone off Treac'h er Goured, where anchoring is forbidden. The Saint-Gildas buoy field fills fast in July and August, so come in by mid-afternoon if you want one.

4.shortDescription: A gentle sail south to Belle-Île, the biggest of the Breton islands, and its busy citadel harbour at Le Palais.
4.description:
Belle-Île lies a short reach south of Houat, close enough that the day feels unhurried. Le Palais announces itself from a distance: the huge star-shaped Citadelle Vauban stands over the harbour mouth. Inside, the port is the largest in southern Brittany and, in high summer, one of the fullest. Expect to raft up on the trot buoys, three or four boats abreast.

Ashore, the town runs on ferries and holidaymakers, but it has real bones. Tour the citadel, hire bikes or a scooter, and strike out for the wild west coast. The Aiguilles de Port-Coton, the rock needles Monet painted, are worth the ride. Provisioning is easy here, with proper shops and a morning market near the quay.
thingsToDo: Tour the Citadelle Vauban above the port
Cycle to the Aiguilles de Port-Coton
Ride out to Grotte de l'Apothicairerie
Shop the morning market by the quay
4.mooringTip: Le Palais moors visitors on trot buoys, rafting several boats to one line, so put out big fenders and expect neighbours. It is the busiest port in the area in July and August; radio ahead or arrive early, and note the outer berths catch the ferry wash.

5.shortDescription: Round the north of Belle-Île to Sauzon, a quieter drying harbour of painted houses, crêpe stalls and Sarah Bernhardt's headland.
5.description:
Sauzon sits barely five miles round the top of the island, so there is no rush. It is everything Le Palais is not: small, calm, easy on the eye, a run of pastel houses stepping up from a narrow inlet. The harbour dries at its head, so most visitors pick up a fore-and-aft mooring in the deeper channel and take the tender ashore.

Spend the afternoon on foot. The quayside stalls do galettes, waffles and Bellilois cider with the harbour laid out below. Walk or cycle out to the Pointe des Poulains, where Sarah Bernhardt kept a house and a small lighthouse marks the island's northern tip. Come back for dinner on the west quay as the light goes soft over the water.
thingsToDo: Eat galettes and cider on the quay
Walk to the Pointe des Poulains lighthouse
See Sarah Bernhardt's clifftop house
Dine on the west harbour quay
5.mooringTip: Sauzon's inner harbour dries, so take a fore-and-aft mooring on the buoys in the deeper channel rather than heading up to the top. The entrance channel runs about 3m deep; check your tide tables before committing to any berth near the drying part.

6.shortDescription: Cross back toward the mainland shore to tiny Hoedic, a car-free speck of dunes, forts and daisy-chain moorings at Argol.
6.description:
The crossing to Hoedic runs a dozen-odd miles northeast across open water, so watch the forecast; the bay kicks up a short sea in a fresh westerly. The island itself is tiny, lower and wilder than its neighbours, ringed by dunes and pale beaches with a single hamlet in the middle.

Port de l'Argol takes you on daisy-chain moorings, three boats to a line, and only smaller yachts fit. Once ashore, an hour or two covers most of it on foot. Walk out to the old Fort d'Hoedic, poke around the chapel, and find a beach to yourself on the eastern shore. There are a couple of simple places to eat near the port, but bring stores; supplies here are thin.
thingsToDo: Walk out to the Fort d'Hoedic
Visit the island chapel and hamlet
Find an empty eastern-shore beach
Eat at a small port bistro
6.mooringTip: Port de l'Argol offers daisy-chain moorings on trot buoys for boats under about 14m and up to 2m draft, so most bareboats fit. It is small and popular; arrive early in season, and be ready to lie close alongside other yachts on the same line.

7.shortDescription: A final reach north across Quiberon Bay and back through the gulf mouth to your berth at Le Crouesty.
7.description:
The run home is a straightforward reach across Quiberon Bay, fifteen miles or so back to the Rhuys peninsula. Time it so you meet the gulf entrance with the flood, or at least at slack; punching a full ebb off Port-Navalo is slow, wet work you can avoid with a little planning.

If the day is kind and you are ahead of schedule, there is time to anchor off the beach at Port-Navalo or drop the hook one last time in the lee of Méaban before turning in. Le Crouesty is a big, well-sheltered marina, easy to enter in almost any weather. Top up fuel and water on the way to your berth, and leave the boat as you would want to find her.
thingsToDo: Anchor off Port-Navalo for a last swim
Time the gulf entrance with the flood
Refuel before returning the boat
Watch the Méaban beacon on the way in
7.mooringTip: Aim to reach the gulf mouth at slack or on the flood; the tide off Port-Navalo runs up to three knots against you on the ebb. Le Crouesty's fuel berth sits near the entrance, so top up before you work back to your allocated pontoon.

riviera-esterel-14-days.metaDesc: A 14-day yacht charter from Cannes along the French Riviera and Esterel coast: Antibes, Monaco, Saint-Tropez, Port-Cros and Porquerolles.

1.shortDescription: A gentle first leg from Cannes to Antibes, home to Europe's biggest yacht harbour and a walled old town worth an afternoon.
1.description: Short and forgiving, this opening leg lets the crew settle before the real distances begin. Cannes to Antibes runs about ten miles, close along the coast, with Cap d'Antibes green and low ahead of you. Port Vauban is the largest marina in Europe, and the superyachts lined along the Quai des Milliardaires make the scale obvious the moment you round Fort Carré. Ashore, the old town rewards an unhurried afternoon. The Marché Provençal on Cours Masséna sells tapenade, socca and Provençal linen under its iron roof most mornings. Above it sits the Château Grimaldi, now the Picasso Museum, where the artist worked in 1946. We usually drop the shore lines by mid-afternoon and walk the ramparts before dinner. A simple day, but a good one to shake the boat down.
thingsToDo: Wander the Marché Provençal on Cours Masséna
Tour the Picasso Museum in Château Grimaldi
Walk the ramparts by Fort Carré
Swim off Plage de la Garoupe, Cap d'Antibes
1.mooringTip: Port Vauban takes visitors, but call the capitainerie ahead in July and August when the big berths fill fast. For a quieter night, anchor in sand off Cap d'Antibes and tender in.

2.shortDescription: Coast east past Nice into one of the Mediterranean's deepest natural bays, where cruise ships and yachts share the same sheltered water.
2.description: From Antibes you track past the airport approach and the long sweep of the Baie des Anges before Nice slides by to port. The Rade de Villefranche opens soon after, one of the deepest natural harbours in the Mediterranean, dropping close to ninety metres between Cap de Nice and Cap Ferrat. The bay holds boats well and stays calm in most conditions, though a strong southerly sends swell straight in, so watch the forecast. The town climbs the hillside in ochre and pink. Rue Obscure, a vaulted medieval passage below the houses, is worth finding, and the baroque Chapelle Saint-Pierre carries decorations by Jean Cocteau. Cap Ferrat next door holds the Villa Ephrussi de Rothschild and its gardens. We often anchor off the beach and take the tender to the fishing quay.
thingsToDo: Find the vaulted Rue Obscure passage
See Cocteau's frescoes in Chapelle Saint-Pierre
Visit Villa Ephrussi de Rothschild on Cap Ferrat
Swim off the Plage des Marinières
2.mooringTip: La Darse marina is small and its berths mostly private, so plan to anchor. Holding is good in sand and mud at eight to twelve metres; take a southerly forecast seriously and move if it builds.

3.shortDescription: A short hop to the Principality, where Port Hercule packs in the superyachts below the casino terraces and the Prince's palace.
3.description: Barely an hour under power, or a lazy sail if there's wind for it. What you remember is the arrival, not the miles. Round Cap Ferrat, pass Cap d'Ail, and Monaco stacks up ahead of you, apartment towers rising straight from the water. Port Hercule has around seven hundred berths but only a handful for visitors, and space in season is scarce and pricey, so booking ahead is close to essential. The Principality repays the effort of getting ashore. The Oceanographic Museum sits on the cliff at Monaco-Ville, its aquarium worth a couple of hours. The old town and the Prince's Palace crown the Rocher, and the Casino de Monte-Carlo needs only a jacket and some nerve. If berths are full, Cap-d'Ail's Port de Fontvieille lies just west and stays calmer.
thingsToDo: Tour the cliffside Oceanographic Museum
Watch the palace guard change at Monaco-Ville
Stroll the Casino de Monte-Carlo square
Walk the exotic garden's cactus terraces
3.mooringTip: Book Port Hercule well ahead in summer; visitor space is limited and rates are steep. Fontvieille, just around the point, is a cheaper and quieter fallback if the main port is full.

4.shortDescription: The turn back west, a longer coastal run past Cap Ferrat and Nice with anchorage choices if the wind stays kind.
4.description: This is the day you unwind the eastern corner and head back toward the Esterel end of the coast. Monaco to Antibes is roughly twenty-five miles, long enough to feel like a proper sail if the afternoon breeze fills in from the southeast. We usually leave by nine to have the wind behind us for the second half. There is no shortage of places to break the run. Beaulieu-sur-Mer and the sheltered water off Cap Ferrat make an easy lunch stop, and the Villa Kerylos, a reconstructed Greek villa on the point, is visible from the deck. Back at Antibes, choose a different berth or anchorage than your first night; the Baie de la Garoupe on the cape's east side is calm in most summer conditions and a short tender from the lighthouse path.
thingsToDo: Lunch stop off Beaulieu-sur-Mer
Spot Villa Kerylos on Cap Ferrat
Anchor in the Baie de la Garoupe
Walk the Sentier du Littoral coastal path
4.mooringTip: The Baie de la Garoupe on Cap d'Antibes gives good sand holding and shelter from anything but an easterly. Arrive before mid-afternoon in July to find swinging room among the day boats.

5.shortDescription: A tiny two-island hop to anchor between the monks' vineyard and the fort that held the Man in the Iron Mask.
5.description: Short enough that you could almost drift there. The run drops you into the channel between Sainte-Marguerite and Saint-Honorat, the two Lérins islands off Cannes. The water between them is a favourite anchorage, sand at five to ten metres with good holding, and it fills with day boats by noon and empties again by evening. Sainte-Marguerite carries the Fort Royal, where legend puts the Man in the Iron Mask, and pine paths cross the island to quiet coves. Saint-Honorat belongs to a Cistercian community that still works its vines; the monks make a wine and the Lérina liqueur, both sold at the abbey shop. Anchoring is banned directly in front of the monastery, marked by yellow buoys, so leave that stretch clear. Stay the night once the trip boats leave and you get the place nearly to yourself.
thingsToDo: Explore Fort Royal on Sainte-Marguerite
Taste Lérina liqueur at Saint-Honorat abbey
Walk the pine paths to quiet coves
Snorkel the shallow channel between the islands
5.mooringTip: Anchor in sand at five to ten metres in the channel, but keep clear of the yellow-buoyed no-anchor zone off the abbey. Booking a park mooring buoy is possible and saves fretting over holding in a blow.

6.shortDescription: Cross to the fiery red Esterel coast and pick up a buoy in Agay's sheltered bay beneath the volcanic hills.
6.description: West again, and the coast changes character completely. The Esterel massif is old volcano, red rhyolite that colours the cliffs and the water at their foot a rusty pink. From the Lérins it is a straightforward run past Théoule and the Pointe de l'Aiguille to Agay, tucked between Cap Dramont and the Pointe de la Baumette. Agay is one of the better natural harbours on this stretch, well protected except from the south. The bay has a buoy field on its eastern side, and because the seagrass beds are protected, taking a buoy rather than dropping the hook is both required in places and simply kinder to the seabed. Cap Dramont, just east, is where Allied troops landed in August 1944, and the little Ile d'Or with its stone tower sits offshore. A calm, red-walled anchorage to end the day.
thingsToDo: Swim beneath the red Esterel cliffs
Walk to the Cap Dramont landing beach
Photograph the Ile d'Or's stone tower
Hike a stretch of the Sentier du Littoral
6.mooringTip: Take a buoy in Agay rather than anchoring; the eastern field is well sheltered and the seagrass beds are protected. Arrive by early afternoon in high season, as the buoys go quickly and are not always bookable.

7.shortDescription: A brief coastal skip to Saint-Raphael, rounding Cap Dramont into a lively resort port with a Byzantine basilica above the quays.
7.description: An easy morning. Saint-Raphael sits only a few miles round Cap Dramont, so there is no rush; the leg is really an excuse to potter along the red coast and swim off the Ile d'Or on the way. The town runs into Fréjus behind it, and the old port gives you a proper marina with fuel, water and a walk into the centre. Saint-Raphael grew as a Belle Époque resort and kept some of that air. The Basilique Notre-Dame-de-la-Victoire, Byzantine in style with a striped dome, stands a few streets back from the water. There is a morning market, a decent fish counter, and enough restaurants along the front to make provisioning painless. A good spot to top up tanks and stock the galley before the longer crossing to the Gulf of Saint-Tropez tomorrow.
thingsToDo: See the Byzantine Basilique Notre-Dame-de-la-Victoire
Browse the Saint-Raphael morning market
Swim off the Ile d'Or on approach
Stock the galley at the port fish counter
7.mooringTip: The Vieux Port at Saint-Raphael and neighbouring Santa Lucia both take visitors and offer fuel and water. Book a night berth in season and use the stop to fill tanks before the open crossing west.

8.shortDescription: Cross the open water to Saint-Tropez, anchoring in the Baie des Canebiers a tender-ride from the citadel and the old port.
8.description: The first real crossing of the trip. From Saint-Raphael you leave the coast and head roughly twenty miles southwest across open water to the Gulf of Saint-Tropez, so pick a settled morning and keep an eye on the afternoon sea breeze, which can build over the gulf. Saint-Tropez itself is expensive and busy in the port, so many crews anchor in the Baie des Canebiers, just east of town. The bottom is sand and grass with good holding, and a buoyed channel keeps swimmers and boats apart. From there it is a short tender to the beach and a half-hour walk past the marine cemetery to the citadel and the old harbour. Place des Lices holds its market on Tuesday and Saturday, and a slice of tarte Tropézienne is close to obligatory. Sénéquier on the quay is for people-watching, not budgets.
thingsToDo: Anchor and swim in Baie des Canebiers
Browse Place des Lices market, Tuesday or Saturday
Climb to the Citadelle for gulf views
Try tarte Tropézienne near the old port
8.mooringTip: Anchor in the Baie des Canebiers in sand and grass at five to ten metres; the port is costly and often full in summer. Keep inside the buoyed channel and leave room to swing when the sea breeze veers.

9.shortDescription: A ten-minute crossing of the gulf to Port Grimaud, a 1960s lakeside town where you moor at your own front door.
9.description: Hardly a passage at all. You cross the head of the gulf to Port Grimaud, the canal town the architect François Spoerry built from scratch in the 1960s. Pastel houses front the water, each with its own mooring, and the whole place is laid out like a small Provençal answer to Venice. The marina has around five hundred berths, with some sixty kept for visitors on boats under twenty metres, so a call ahead in season is sensible. Once you are tied up, the town is all footbridges and quiet quays, best seen with an evening walk and a coche d'eau, the little electric ferries that thread the canals. Climb to the old hilltop village of Grimaud behind it for the wider view, castle ruins and a Templar chapel included. An easy, pretty night after the crossing.
thingsToDo: Ride a coche d'eau through the canals
Walk to hilltop Grimaud's castle ruins
Cross the footbridges at golden hour
Eat at a canal-side terrace
9.mooringTip: Port Grimaud keeps around sixty visitor berths for boats under twenty metres; reserve ahead in July and August. Approach slowly and watch the shallow, marked entrance channel, which silts on the edges.

10.shortDescription: Round the wild capes south of the gulf to Cavalaire's broad sandy bay at the foot of the Pradels hills.
10.description: Leaving the gulf, you round the string of protected headlands that make this one of the prettiest corners of the Var coast. Cap Camarat with its lighthouse, then Cap Taillat and Cap Lardier, all part of a shoreline kept free of building. Slip in behind them for a swim; the Plage de l'Escalet and the water off Cap Taillat run clear and quiet on a calm day. Cavalaire-sur-Mer sits at the end of the run, a wide bay under the Massif des Pradels with a long sandy beach and a modern marina. It is an unpretentious resort after the gloss of Saint-Tropez, good for restocking and a relaxed evening. The bay opens to the south and east, so it is a fair-weather anchorage; the marina is the safer bet if any swell is forecast.
thingsToDo: Swim off Cap Taillat's sand spit
Anchor at Plage de l'Escalet
Restock at Cavalaire's seafront shops
Walk the protected Cap Lardier trail
10.mooringTip: Cavalaire's bay is open to south and east, so anchor only in settled weather and take the marina if swell threatens. Off Cap Taillat the holding is good in sand, but it is a daytime stop, not an overnight one in wind.

11.shortDescription: Follow the Maures coast to Le Lavandou, a working seaside town with a dozen beaches and the Golden Islands offshore.
11.description: A relaxed coastal leg under the Massif des Maures, the dark wooded hills that back this shore. Past Cap Nègre and the discreet villas along its flanks, then the string of coves the locals call the twelve beaches of Le Lavandou. Cavalière is the biggest, with room to anchor off the sand in settled weather and swim ashore for lunch. Le Lavandou is a real fishing and holiday town rather than a set piece, and better for it. The port takes visitors, the market is genuine, and the seafront has the kind of unfussy restaurants where the catch is local. From here the Iles d'Or lie just across the water, so it makes a natural staging point before you cross to Port-Cros. We often provision here for the two island nights, since the parks have little for sale.
thingsToDo: Anchor and swim off Cavalière beach
Provision at Le Lavandou's market
Eat fresh catch on the seafront
Sail past the villas of Cap Nègre
11.mooringTip: Le Lavandou's port takes visitors and is a good place to top up before the Golden Islands, where supplies are thin. In an offshore breeze you can anchor off Cavalière beach; move in if the wind swings onshore.

12.shortDescription: Cross to Port-Cros, Europe's first marine national park, and pick up a park buoy off a forested, car-free island.
12.description: A short crossing takes you to Port-Cros, the wildest of the three Golden Islands and the heart of a national park set up in 1963, among the first marine reserves in Europe. The whole island is protected, thickly wooded, and free of cars, and the rules on the water are strict for good reason. Moorings come from a park buoy field, sized for boats up to fifteen metres, and anchoring is banned in the Passe de Bagaud to protect the seagrass. Your boat needs a holding tank for black water to use these waters. Once settled, the walking is the draw: the path to the Fort de l'Estissac and the shaded trail across to the Plage de la Palud, where a marked snorkel trail runs the shallows past the Rascas islet. Quiet, green, and a world away from the Saint-Tropez quays.
thingsToDo: Snorkel the marked trail off Plage de la Palud
Climb to the Fort de l'Estissac
Walk the shaded path across the island
Watch for grouper in the clear shallows
12.mooringTip: Use a national-park buoy; anchoring is restricted and forbidden in the Passe de Bagaud. Buoys suit boats up to fifteen metres and a black-water holding tank is required, so arrive early and have the tank sorted.

13.shortDescription: Hop to Porquerolles, the largest Golden Island, for white-sand beaches, vineyard lanes and a village square shaded by eucalyptus.
13.description: Porquerolles lies a gentle few miles west, the biggest and most inhabited of the islands. The village grew around a nineteenth-century garrison, and its square, dusty with pétanque and shaded by eucalyptus, still feels like a frontier outpost that decided to stay for the wine. The north coast holds the best-known beaches, Plage Notre-Dame and Plage d'Argent among them, both sand and both sheltered from the mistral behind the island. Anchor off the north shore and you are a short walk or bike ride from the village; the island has vineyards making Côtes de Provence rosé and lanes easily covered on a rented bike. Fort Sainte-Agathe looks down over the harbour, and the lighthouse walk at the south end gives you the cliffs and the open sea. The most polished of the three islands, and a fine last anchorage.
thingsToDo: Swim at Plage Notre-Dame or Plage d'Argent
Rent a bike through the vineyard lanes
Taste Côtes de Provence rosé at a domaine
Walk to the southern lighthouse cliffs
13.mooringTip: Anchor off the north-coast beaches, sheltered from the mistral, in sand at five to twelve metres. Plage Notre-Dame and d'Argent fill with day boats by late morning, so drop the hook early to claim swinging room.

14.shortDescription: The long final leg back to Cannes, an early start across open water to close the loop on the Croisette.
14.description: The longest day of the fortnight, and one to plan properly. Porquerolles back to Cannes is a big stretch of open water, well over forty miles, so we usually slip the buoy at first light to be in before the afternoon breeze hardens. If the crew wants to break it, Le Lavandou or Cavalaire make sensible halfway stops. The coast rebuilds as you go, the Maures giving way to the red Esterel and finally the low green of Cap d'Antibes and the Lérins. Cannes closes the circle in style, La Croisette running gold along the front and Le Suquet, the old town, climbing to its church tower and the view back over the bay. Berth in the Vieux Port or anchor off the Croisette for a last night, and toast a fortnight's sailing well done.
thingsToDo: Walk La Croisette and the old harbour
Climb Le Suquet for the bay view
Browse Marché Forville for last provisions
Toast the trip from the Croisette anchorage
14.mooringTip: Leave Porquerolles at dawn for this long leg and watch the afternoon breeze off the Esterel. In Cannes, the Vieux Port takes visitors but is busy; anchoring off La Croisette in settled weather is a pleasant, cheaper last night.
